incidents.headline: Iran War & Strait of Hormuz closure — JLR's aluminium baseline re-prices overnight
incidents.summary: On 28 February 2026, US and Israeli forces began coordinated strikes against Iranian nuclear and ballistic-missile infrastructure. Iran's supreme leader was killed in the strikes. On 4 March, Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed and began attacking transiting vessels. UK Maritime Trade Operations recorded 10 attacks by 8 March; five crew on two vessels killed. Commercial traffic through the Strait dropped >90%. Brent moved from $72.87 to $112 inside three weeks. Closure of 27% of the world's maritime crude trade. QatarEnergy declared force majeure on all LNG exports. Kuwait, Iraq, Saudi Arabia and UAE collectively lost 6.7M barrels/day by 10 March, rising to ~10M by 12 March. Gulf aluminium smelters declared force majeure on deliveries. European industrial gas prices roughly doubled. JLR's FY24/25 register treats geopolitics and supply chain as independent lines. Hormuz collapses that distinction: a single geopolitical event re-prices the entire aluminium baseline, the energy cost-curve for every Tier-1 in Europe, and the assumption that Range Rover line-cost is stable from FY26 budgets onward. The register names both risks; it does not name the dependency between them. The 57% Brent move in three weeks moved faster than JLR's quarterly forecast cycle — meaning operational hedging instruments designed for incremental risk are the wrong instruments for this kind of step-change.

graded_claims.claim: US and Israeli forces began coordinated strikes against Iranian nuclear and ballistic-missile infrastructure on 28 February 2026.
Iran's supreme leader was killed in the strikes.
On 4 March 2026, Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed and began attacking transiting vessels.
UK Maritime Trade Operations recorded 10 attacks by 8 March 2026; five crew on two vessels killed.
Commercial traffic through the Strait dropped more than 90%.
Brent crude moved from $72.87 to $112 inside three weeks — a 57% move.
QatarEnergy declared force majeure on all LNG exports.
Kuwait, Iraq, Saudi Arabia and UAE collectively lost 6.7M barrels/day by 10 March 2026, rising to ~10M by 12 March.
Gulf aluminium smelters declared force majeure on deliveries.
European industrial gas prices roughly doubled.
JLR's FY24/25 risk register treats geopolitics and supply chain as independent lines, not naming the dependency between them.
Approximately 25% of European automotive suppliers are anticipating a financial deficit in 2026.
Finding and qualifying an alternative aluminium supplier typically takes up to 18 months.
More than 150,000 tonnes of aluminium registered on the LME have been pulled from warehouses.
The Strait of Hormuz carries approximately 27% of the world's maritime crude trade.

internal.name: JLR Manufacturing — Solihull Body-in-White & Aluminium Intensive Vehicle Programme
internal.reason: Solihull AIV line uses aerospace-grade aluminium sheet; Gulf force majeure directly re-prices Range Rover BIW cost base.
internal.impact_rationale: JLR's aluminium-intensive Range Rover platform sourced ~14% EU aluminium from Gulf; force majeure disrupts FY26 budget baseline.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Convene a war-room within 48h to map every active Gulf aluminium call-off order and identify covered vs. uncovered volume.

internal.name: JLR Group Treasury & Commodity Hedging Desk
internal.reason: JLR's quarterly-cycle hedging instruments cannot absorb a 57% Brent step-change in three weeks; existing hedge book is structurally mis-sized for this shock.
internal.impact_rationale: 57% Brent move in three weeks outpaced quarterly hedge-roll cycle; aluminium LME premium exposure unhedged.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Stress-test existing commodity hedge book against $112 Brent sustained for six months and brief Board on mark-to-market exposure by end of week.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure JLR's commodity cost baseline and operational continuity planning account for geopolitical events that simultaneously re-price multiple input categories via a single chokepoint dependency.
Maintain ongoing situational awareness of geopolitical conditions in the Strait of Hormuz corridor that materially affect JLR's aluminium and energy procurement exposure.
Ensure JLR's scenario-planning framework captures step-change geopolitical shocks — not merely incremental drift — that invalidate FY26 budget assumptions within a single quarter.
Ensure JLR's Tier-1 supply base for aluminium and energy-intensive components is mapped against single-chokepoint concentration risk, with active mitigation plans in place.
Maintain JLR's operational continuity across Solihull AIV and Castle Bromwich lines under scenarios in which Gulf aluminium supply is disrupted for more than 30 days.
Ensure JLR's business continuity plans are tested against compound shocks in which geopolitical disruption simultaneously hits materials cost, energy cost, and logistics availability.
Ensure JLR's commodity hedging programme is sized and structured to absorb step-change price moves — such as a 57% Brent increase in three weeks — not only incremental quarterly variance.
Maintain JLR's financial resilience under scenarios in which the aluminium cost baseline reprices materially above FY26 budget assumptions for a sustained period.
Ensure JLR's Tier-1 and Tier-2 supplier concentration in Gulf-proximate aluminium smelting is disclosed, monitored, and subject to active contingency contracting.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain a live Hormuz scenario monitor feeding JLR's
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Risk Officer must maintain a live Hormuz scenario monitor — drawing on UK Maritime Trade Operations vessel-attack feeds and LME aluminium warehouse drawdown data — that updates JLR's quarterly commodity-cost model within 24 hours of any new vessel attack, closure escalation, or force majeure declaration post-4 March 2026; the updated model output must be distributed simultaneously to the Procurement and Treasury desks on each trigger event. Evidence of completion is a dated distribution log showing each model refresh, the triggering event it responded to, and the named recipients in Procurement and Treasury.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a monitoring feed; this adaptation names the specific data sources (UKMTO attack log, LME drawdown register), the specific cost model it must feed (JLR's quarterly commodity-cost model), and the 24-hour SLA anchored to the 4 March 2026 closure date — preventing the 57% Brent step-change from again outpacing JLR's internal forecasting cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Integrate the Strait of Hormuz closure scenario —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Before the FY27 planning cycle opens, JLR's Chief Risk Officer must present to the Board Risk Committee a revised geopolitical risk appetite statement that explicitly names the Strait of Hormuz as a Severity-5 chokepoint scenario, references the 4 March 2026 Iranian closure declaration and the resulting >90% commercial traffic drop as the calibrating event, and sets quantified tolerance thresholds for aluminium unit-cost variance and Brent exposure that the business will accept before mandating operational response; the revised statement must be Board-ratified and incorporated into the FY27 planning assumptions pack. Evidence of completion is the signed Board Risk Committee minute recording ratification and the FY27 planning pack version that references the Hormuz scenario by name.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires integration of a closure scenario into risk appetite; this adaptation anchors the calibration to the specific 4 March 2026 declaration, requires quantified aluminium-cost and Brent tolerances rather than qualitative language, and mandates that those thresholds appear inside the FY27 planning pack — ensuring the scenario shapes budget assumptions rather than sitting only in the risk register.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a JLR Board-level escalation trigger: if Brent
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Treasury Director must configure a standing Board-level escalation trigger — effective immediately and documented in the Treasury risk procedures manual — that automatically convenes an emergency commodity-risk session of the JLR Board Audit and Risk Committee within 48 hours whenever Brent crude moves more than 20% within any rolling 10-trading-day window, using the 28 February–21 March 2026 57% move from $72.87 to $112 as the calibrating precedent; the session agenda must include current aluminium hedge-book exposure, Solihull AIV production cost-at-risk, and a Treasury recommendation on instrument adjustment. Evidence of completion is the documented trigger rule in the Treasury procedures manual, signed by the Treasury Director, and the log of any sessions convened under it.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control sets a 20%/10-day Brent trigger; this adaptation calibrates that threshold explicitly against the March 2026 $72.87-to-$112 move, mandates that the emergency session agenda address AIV aluminium exposure specifically, and requires the rule to be embedded in the Treasury procedures manual — closing the gap whereby JLR's quarterly forecast cycle was too slow to respond to a three-week step-change.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Commission a cross-category dependency analysis mapping JLR's GEO-CFL
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Risk Officer must commission, within 60 days of this control's issuance, a formal cross-category dependency analysis that maps every GEO-CFL and GEO-POL entry in the FY24/25 risk register to a common Hormuz chokepoint node, explicitly documenting how the 4 March 2026 Iranian closure declaration simultaneously triggered the Gulf aluminium smelter force majeure declarations and the European industrial gas price doubling — thereby evidencing that the two risk lines share a single root cause; the completed analysis must recommend register restructuring for FY27 and be approved by the Chief Risk Officer before the FY27 register is locked. Evidence of completion is the signed dependency analysis report and the FY27 register showing a merged or explicitly linked GEO-CFL/supply-chain entry referencing the Hormuz chokepoint.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a dependency mapping; this adaptation names the specific register vintage (FY24/25), the specific dual failure (aluminium force majeure + European gas doubling) that both trace to the 4 March closure, and mandates that the output changes register architecture before FY27 is locked — preventing the false independence from carrying forward into the next planning cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Appoint a named geopolitical intelligence lead within JLR
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Risk Officer must appoint, within 30 days, a named Geopolitical Intelligence Lead — a dedicated role within the JLR risk function — who is accountable for delivering a weekly written Hormuz corridor threat assessment to the heads of Procurement and Treasury every Monday by 09:00 UK time; each assessment must address current Strait transit status (referencing UKMTO incident logs), Brent trajectory against the $112 March 2026 peak, Gulf aluminium smelter operational status, and any new force majeure declarations by Gulf or LNG suppliers. Evidence of completion is the appointment letter, the role mandate document, and a continuous weekly distribution log.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a geopolitical intelligence lead; this adaptation specifies the weekly cadence and Monday 09:00 delivery deadline, names the four mandatory content elements anchored to the March 2026 event (UKMTO status, Brent vs $112 benchmark, smelter status, new force majeures), and ensures Procurement and Treasury receive synchronised intelligence rather than relying on ad-hoc market feeds.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Identify and pre-qualify at least two non-Gulf aluminium
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Procurement must identify and complete technical pre-qualification of at least two aluminium sheet suppliers outside the Gulf region — capable of supplying Range Rover AIV-specification aluminium alloy (AA6111 and AA5754 grades or equivalent Solihull-approved substitutes) — within 90 days of this control's issuance, with each supplier contractually confirmed as able to activate delivery to Solihull within 60 days of a Gulf force majeure declaration equivalent to that issued by Gulf smelters on or after 4 March 2026; pre-qualification must include a documented technical assessment against JLR's AIV body-in-white specification and a confirmed logistics routing that does not transit the Strait of Hormuz. Evidence of completion is two signed pre-qualification certificates, two activation-ready supply agreements, and a logistics-route audit for each supplier.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires non-Gulf source identification; this adaptation names the specific alloy grades required for the Range Rover AIV programme, anchors the 60-day activation window to the 4 March 2026 Gulf force majeure baseline, and explicitly requires that logistics routings exclude the Hormuz Strait — preventing a repeat scenario where the alternative route replicates the original chokepoint exposure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish minimum 60-day strategic aluminium inventory buffer at
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Operations must establish, within 120 days, a minimum 60-day strategic aluminium inventory buffer at the Solihull manufacturing facility covering all AIV-programme grades (including AA6111 body-side and AA5754 closure panel sheet), sized against the average monthly call-off volumes for the Range Rover and Range Rover Sport AIV lines as of Q4 FY26; the buffer level must be reviewed monthly by the Solihull Supply Chain Director and must trigger a replenishment alert at 45 days of remaining cover, calibrated against a disruption duration at least as long as the Gulf force majeure event declared on or after 4 March 2026. Evidence of completion is a signed inventory policy document, an initial stock confirmation report showing 60-day cover achieved, and a monthly dashboard showing current days-of-cover against the 60-day floor.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a strategic buffer; this adaptation names the specific Solihull facility, the specific AIV alloy grades, the 45-day replenishment trigger, and anchors the buffer duration to the 4 March 2026 Gulf force majeure event — ensuring the buffer is sized against a real precedent disruption rather than an arbitrary planning assumption.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Test JLR's supplier communication protocol against a scenario
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supply Chain Risk must design and execute a tabletop exercise within 90 days — involving Procurement, the Solihull plant director, Treasury, and the top five energy-intensive Tier-1 aluminium suppliers — that simulates the simultaneous invocation of force majeure by multiple Gulf aluminium smelters and a European industrial gas supplier within a 72-hour window, modelled on the conditions of the week of 4 March 2026; the exercise must test JLR's supplier communication protocol end-to-end, identify response latency gaps, and produce a remediation action log with named owners and completion dates. Evidence of completion is the exercise scenario pack, an attendance register, and a signed remediation action log reviewed by the Chief Supply Chain Officer.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires protocol testing; this adaptation models the exercise explicitly on the 4 March 2026 compound event (Gulf smelter force majeure + European gas doubling), names the internal and supplier participants, and requires a post-exercise remediation log — converting a test into a traceable improvement cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update JLR's business continuity plan to explicitly address
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Operating Officer must commission an update to JLR's Business Continuity Plan, to be completed within 60 days, that inserts a dedicated compound-scenario annex addressing the simultaneous occurrence of Gulf aluminium smelter force majeure (as declared on or after 4 March 2026) and a doubling of European industrial gas prices (as reported in March 2026), including defined response playbooks for Solihull AIV production continuity, Tier-1 supplier financial support triggers, and cross-functional decision rights; the annex must be approved by the JLR Executive Committee and version-controlled as BCP Annex: Hormuz Compound Event. Evidence of completion is the signed BCP annex, the Executive Committee approval minute, and its inclusion in the live BCP document under version control.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires BCP updating for compound scenarios; this adaptation names the two specific co-occurring failures from March 2026 (Gulf force majeure + European gas doubling), requires a dedicated named annex rather than an edit to existing text, and specifies that response playbooks must address Solihull AIV production continuity — targeting the specific asset at risk.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define clear production-stop decision criteria for Solihull AIV
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Operations and VP Procurement must jointly define and document, within 45 days, explicit production-stop decision criteria for the Solihull AIV line triggered when aluminium call-off orders cannot be fulfilled for more than 14 consecutive calendar days under Gulf disruption conditions equivalent to those following the 4 March 2026 force majeure declaration; the criteria must specify the decision authority (minimum: Chief Operating Officer sign-off), the sequenced wind-down steps for the Range Rover and Range Rover Sport AIV lines, the financial exposure threshold that triggers Board notification, and the conditions under which production restart is authorised. Evidence of completion is a signed decision-criteria document, incorporated into the Solihull plant operating procedures and cross-referenced in the BCP Hormuz Compound Event annex.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires production-stop criteria; this adaptation names the 14-day threshold, the specific Solihull AIV lines at risk (Range Rover and Range Rover Sport), the required decision authority level, and the restart conditions — providing an unambiguous operational decision tree rather than a general principle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require JLR Treasury to stress-test the existing aluminium
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Treasury Director must, within 30 days, deliver to the Audit and Risk Committee a written stress-test report on JLR's current aluminium and Brent crude hedge book, modelling a sustained $112/bbl Brent price environment for two full fiscal quarters (Q1 and Q2 FY27) — using the 28 February to 21 March 2026 move from $72.87 to $112 as the entry scenario — and quantifying hedge-book P&L impact, unhedged aluminium cost exposure for the Solihull AIV programme, and the point at which existing instruments reach their cover limits; the report must include a Treasury recommendation on whether to extend tenor, increase cover ratio, or switch instrument type. Evidence of completion is the dated stress-test report tabled at an Audit and Risk Committee meeting and the signed Committee minute recording the recommendation received.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a stress test; this adaptation uses the actual March 2026 Brent trajectory ($72.87 to $112) as the scenario entry point, specifies Q1 and Q2 FY27 as the two-quarter window, requires the report to quantify AIV-programme-specific aluminium exposure, and mandates a Treasury instrument recommendation — making the output actionable rather than informational.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review JLR's commodity hedging instrument selection to determine
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Treasury Director must complete, within 45 days, a formal hedging instrument review comparing the cost and coverage profile of JLR's current quarterly forward contracts against Asian-barrier options and zero-cost collars for both Brent crude and primary aluminium, using the 57% three-week Brent move of February–March 2026 as the stress scenario for which quarterly forwards provided inadequate response time; the review must assess each instrument's ability to provide meaningful protection within a 10-trading-day step-change window and present a recommended instrument mix to the Audit and Risk Committee for approval before Q2 FY27 hedging positions are placed. Evidence of completion is the instrument review document and the signed Audit and Risk Committee minute recording approval of the recommended instrument mix.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires instrument review; this adaptation names the specific instruments to be compared (quarterly forwards vs Asian-barrier options vs zero-cost collars), uses the exact February–March 2026 57% Brent move as the test scenario, and sets a deadline tied to Q2 FY27 position-placing — ensuring the review changes actual hedging behaviour before the next exposure window opens.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether the Hormuz aluminium repricing event meets
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's General Counsel and Group Financial Controller must, within 15 business days of this control's issuance, complete and document a written UK Market Abuse Regulation Article 7 materiality assessment determining whether the Gulf aluminium smelter force majeure declarations (on or after 4 March 2026), the >150,000-tonne LME aluminium drawdown, and the resulting repricing of JLR's aluminium cost baseline constitute inside information requiring prompt disclosure via Tata Motors' Bombay Stock Exchange filing obligations; if the assessment concludes the threshold is met, a draft BSE disclosure must be prepared and submitted to Tata Motors' Company Secretary within five further business days. Evidence of completion is the signed materiality assessment document, and either the filed BSE disclosure or a signed legal opinion confirming non-disclosure is appropriate.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a MAR materiality assessment; this adaptation names the specific triggering events (Gulf smelter force majeure, LME drawdown exceeding 150,000 tonnes), the specific disclosure route (BSE filing via Tata Motors), and the sequential 15+5 business-day deadline — providing a traceable compliance workflow rather than a general assessment obligation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require all Tier-1 aluminium suppliers — including Novelis
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Procurement must issue formal written information requests to all Tier-1 aluminium suppliers — including Novelis and Hydro Aluminium — within 15 business days of this control's issuance, requiring each supplier to disclose in writing within 30 days: the percentage of their primary aluminium feedstock sourced from Gulf smelters affected by the force majeure declarations of or after 4 March 2026, their own inventory cover in days as at the date of response, and the specific contractual or operational thresholds that would trigger their own force majeure notice to JLR; responses must be reviewed by Procurement and consolidated into a Gulf-concentration risk register for Tier-1 aluminium suppliers, presented to the Chief Supply Chain Officer within 60 days. Evidence of completion is the dated information requests, supplier response log, and the signed consolidated risk register.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires supplier Gulf-concentration disclosure; this adaptation names Novelis and Hydro Aluminium explicitly, specifies the three required disclosure elements (feedstock sourcing %, inventory cover, force majeure trigger thresholds), anchors the request to the 4 March 2026 smelter declarations, and requires consolidation into a named risk register — creating a structured supply-chain visibility output rather than a collection of unprocessed disclosures.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Insert explicit Hormuz-closure force majeure language and step-in
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Procurement and General Counsel must, within 60 days, develop and approve standard contractual clauses — to be inserted into all new and renewing JLR aluminium supply agreements from Q2 FY27 onward — that explicitly define a Hormuz Closure Event as a named force majeure category (referencing the 4 March 2026 Iranian declaration and >90% Strait traffic drop as the definitional precedent), specify JLR's step-in rights including the right to activate pre-qualified alternative suppliers within 30 days of a force majeure notice, and require suppliers to maintain minimum inventory cover of 45 days at all times; these clauses must be reviewed by external shipping and commodities counsel before finalisation. Evidence of completion is the approved standard clause set, the external counsel sign-off letter, and confirmation of insertion in the first contract renewed or executed under Q2 FY27 terms.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires Hormuz force majeure language in contracts; this adaptation defines the specific Hormuz Closure Event trigger using the 4 March 2026 declaration as the definitional precedent, specifies the 30-day step-in activation window and the 45-day supplier inventory floor, and requires external commodities counsel review — ensuring the clauses are legally enforceable in the context of a defined, precedented event rather than relying on generic force majeure boilerplate.

incidents.headline: Trump's 25% on EU autos — JLR's competitive advantage above the 100,000 cap
incidents.summary: President Trump announced 1 May 2026 a 25% Section 232 tariff on EU-origin cars and trucks, citing EU non-compliance with the Turnberry Agreement. The exemption for US-produced units stands. JLR's US-UK 10% deal (May 2025) holds for the first 100,000 vehicles annually. JLR shipped 101,000 vehicles to the US in 2024 — the cap is already exceeded. Mercedes-Benz, BMW and Volkswagen carry the greatest direct exposure on EU-built US-bound vehicles. VDA called for de-escalation. The EU-US deal was expected to save European automakers €500–600M per month under Turnberry; that benefit is now being unwound. The 25% Section 232 is the first 2026 event where JLR has positional advantage by accident of UK-US deal architecture. Mercedes, BMW, Porsche, Ferrari now carry the full tariff on US-bound vehicles. JLR's 100K-vehicle cap, however, is structural: at 101,000 vehicles to the US in 2024, the marginal vehicle is already above-cap exposure at 25% landed cost. The strategic question is not whether the tariff hurts JLR (it doesn't, relatively) — it's whether the UK-US cap holds and how JLR rations US allocation if it doesn't.

graded_claims.claim: President Trump announced on 1 May 2026 a 25% Section 232 tariff on EU-origin cars and trucks, citing EU non-compliance with the Turnberry Agreement.
The UK-US deal (May 2025) provides JLR a 10% tariff rate on the first 100,000 vehicles shipped annually to the US.
JLR shipped 101,000 vehicles to the US in 2024, meaning the 100,000-vehicle cap is already structurally exceeded.
Mercedes-Benz, BMW, and Volkswagen carry the greatest direct exposure on EU-built US-bound vehicles.
Bernstein estimates BMW EBIT impact of -12% to -15%, Mercedes -14% to -18%, Porsche -16% to -21%, VW -9% to -11%.
The EU-US Turnberry Agreement was expected to save European automakers €500–600M per month; that benefit is now being unwound.
VDA called for de-escalation following the 25% tariff announcement.
The July 4, 2026 deadline is described as a live ratification deadline for the 25% EU auto tariff.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Maintain continuous monitoring of bilateral trade agreement provisions — specifically the UK-US 100K-vehicle exemption threshold — and quantify exposure on above-cap volumes at the operative Section 232 rate of 25%.

Identify and track geopolitical developments — including US-EU Turnberry Agreement compliance status and July 4, 2026 ratification deadline — that may materially alter JLR's competitive tariff advantage.
Assess country-of-origin and trade-agreement stability risk for JLR's UK manufacturing base given that JLR's 10% rate depends entirely on the durability of the May 2025 UK-US bilateral instrument.
Evaluate competitive repositioning options available to JLR in the US luxury SUV segment given the 15-percentage-point tariff differential advantage over EU-origin German OEM competitors.
Develop scenario-based strategic plans for US market allocation, pricing, and product mix under conditions where the 100K-vehicle cap is maintained, renegotiated, or removed.
Quantify the incremental landed-cost increase on each US-bound vehicle above the 100,000-unit threshold under the 25% Section 232 rate and model impact on JLR US contribution margin.
Assess treasury and cash-flow risk arising from tariff-driven cost increases on above-cap units and model working capital implications across FY2026 and FY2027 US shipment plans.
Manage JLR's stakeholder communications regarding its relative tariff advantage to avoid reputational exposure from being perceived as a 'winner' in a politically sensitive trade dispute.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must maintain a live treaty-monitoring protocol for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Government Affairs Director (UK Trade Policy) must maintain a dedicated monitoring protocol for the May 2025 UK-US bilateral instrument, tracking all USTR Federal Register notices, DBT ministerial statements, and UK Parliament trade committee publications for any language signalling renegotiation or suspension of the 100,000-vehicle annual cap or the 10% preferential rate, with a mandatory escalation to the Chief Legal Officer within 24 hours of any such signal. Given that the July 4, 2026 EU tariff ratification deadline creates a live renegotiation window during which the UK deal's architecture may be scrutinised for equity by USTR, monitoring cadence must be daily from 1 June 2026 through 5 July 2026. Evidence of completion is a maintained monitoring log with dated entries, a documented escalation matrix naming the responsible Government Affairs Director, and a board-level confirmation receipt if escalation is triggered.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires treaty monitoring in general terms; this adaptation specifies the July 4, 2026 EU tariff ratification deadline as the precise high-risk window during which USTR scrutiny of the UK-US cap architecture is most likely, and mandates daily rather than periodic monitoring over that defined 35-day corridor.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must model financial exposure under at least
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Finance and Strategy function must produce and board-approve a structured financial exposure model covering three discrete scenarios anchored to the confirmed 101,000-vehicle 2024 US shipment baseline: (a) cap held — first 100,000 units at 10%, the 1,000 above-cap units at 25% Section 232; (b) cap revised — all US-bound units from the date of any USTR announcement bearing the full 25% Section 232 rate; and (c) full bilateral deal collapse — all US-bound volumes reverting to MFN treatment with no preferential rate. Each scenario must include per-unit landed-cost deltas by nameplate, aggregate EBIT impact for FY2026 and FY2027, and break-even volume thresholds, with the model updated in the quarter immediately following any material USTR or DBT statement and no less than quarterly thereafter. The model output must be presented to the JLR Board Audit and Risk Committee with a signed-off version retained as evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control specifies three scenarios in outline; this adaptation locks them to JLR's confirmed 101,000-unit 2024 shipment figure and the 1,000-unit structural overage as the baseline, and ties the update cadence explicitly to USTR/DBT trigger events as well as the standard quarterly cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish an escalation protocol triggered if
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR must implement a named escalation protocol — designated 'Protocol CAPWATCH' — that is automatically triggered upon any public USTR statement, Federal Register notice, or credible media report citing revision, suspension, or renegotiation of the May 2025 UK-US automotive deal or its 100,000-vehicle cap, requiring the Chief Strategy Officer to convene a board-level US Allocation Strategy Review within 48 hours of the trigger event. The review must produce, within 72 hours of convening, a decision record on whether to pause above-cap US shipments, accelerate sub-cap allocations, or hedge tariff exposure through contractual price adjustment clauses; the decision record must be signed by the CEO and retained as evidence of completion. Protocol CAPWATCH must be documented, distributed to the US Commercial Director and CFO, and tested via a tabletop exercise before 30 June 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a board escalation within 48 hours; this adaptation names the protocol, identifies the specific trigger conditions tied to the May 2025 UK-US deal and its cap, specifies the three decision options the review must resolve, and mandates a pre-July 4 tabletop test given the live ratification deadline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's geopolitical risk register must be updated to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Risk Officer must update the Corporate Geopolitical Risk Register by 15 June 2026 to include a dedicated risk entry — 'UK-US Cap Contingency Risk' — that records the Section 232 25% EU-origin tariff as creating a conditional competitive advantage for JLR that is structurally contingent on two specific conditions remaining intact: (i) the UK retaining its non-EU trade classification under US Section 232 determinations, and (ii) the 100,000-vehicle annual cap and 10% rate in the May 2025 bilateral instrument not being revised. The risk entry must assign a likelihood and impact rating under both conditions, name the risk owner (Government Affairs Director), specify the two monitoring signals that would trigger a risk rating upgrade (any USTR reclassification of UK origin treatment; any DBT or USTR statement on cap revision), and be reviewed at each quarterly risk committee meeting. Evidence of completion is the dated register entry, sign-off by the CRO, and inclusion in the next Board Risk Committee pack.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a register update for the competitive advantage; this adaptation makes explicit that the advantage has two distinct structural dependencies — UK origin classification and cap integrity — each of which constitutes a separate risk condition requiring its own monitoring signal, rather than treating the advantage as a single undifferentiated entry.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must assess whether the UK-US deal's 100K-vehicle
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Legal and Trade Compliance function, supported by external UK trade counsel, must produce a formal legal opinion by 30 June 2026 assessing whether the 100,000-vehicle cap and 10% preferential rate in the May 2025 UK-US bilateral instrument contain any Most Favoured Nation (MFN) obligations or reciprocal-treatment clauses that could be invoked by the EU, Japan, South Korea, or other US bilateral automotive partners to demand equivalent cap-and-rate treatment, and whether the EU's exposure to the 25% Section 232 tariff creates a legal or political basis for any such demand. The opinion must specifically address whether EU or WTO MFN disciplines apply to the US-UK deal structure, identify any treaty text that could be cited by a third-party government, and recommend whether JLR should proactively engage DBT on this question before the July 4, 2026 ratification deadline. The completed legal opinion, signed by external counsel, constitutes evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an MFN implication assessment; this adaptation specifies the July 4, 2026 deadline as the urgency driver, names the EU as the most immediately relevant party given its confirmed 25% Section 232 exposure, and requires the opinion to assess both legal enforceability and political invocation risk as distinct dimensions.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's US commercial team must develop a model-mix
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's US Commercial Director must, by the next US shipment cycle following 1 May 2026, produce and gain CFO sign-off on a formal Model-Mix Rationing Framework that allocates the 100,000 sub-cap annual slots exclusively to JLR's highest average selling price nameplates — specifically Range Rover Long Wheelbase, Range Rover Sport, and Defender 130 — and excludes lower-ASP configurations from sub-cap allocation until cap headroom exists. The framework must specify the per-nameplate slot volumes, a reallocation trigger if any nameplate underperforms its US sales forecast by more than 10%, and a reconciliation process to ensure the 100,000-unit threshold is not breached in the shipping manifest submitted to US Customs, given that JLR's 2024 US shipment volume of 101,000 units confirms there is zero structural headroom. Evidence of completion is the signed framework document, the nameplate allocation table, and confirmation from the US logistics team that customs manifest controls are updated to enforce the 100,000-unit ceiling.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a rationing framework favouring high-ASP nameplates; this adaptation enforces that the framework must treat the 100,000-unit ceiling as a hard customs-manifest control — not a planning target — because JLR's confirmed 2024 overage of 1,000 units means any planning imprecision directly triggers 25% Section 232 exposure on marginal vehicles.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must prepare a US pricing strategy that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's US Pricing and Revenue Management team, in coordination with the Chief Commercial Officer and external US trade counsel, must develop by 31 July 2026 a documented US pricing strategy that captures a portion of the 15-percentage-point tariff differential between JLR's confirmed 10% rate and the 25% Section 232 rate applied to EU-origin competitors including Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Porsche, and Volkswagen, implemented through MSRP or configuration adjustments on sub-cap units rather than through any public communication that attributes price positioning to tariff advantage. The strategy document must include a USTR scrutiny risk assessment confirming that the pricing approach does not rely on or publicise the preferential treatment in a manner that could invite USTR review of the UK-US deal structure, and must set a maximum price capture percentage that legal counsel confirms is defensible. Evidence of completion is the signed pricing strategy document, the legal sign-off on USTR scrutiny risk, and updated US market pricing guidelines distributed to the JLR US retail network.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a pricing strategy capturing tariff differential without triggering USTR scrutiny; this adaptation makes explicit that the communication risk is as material as the pricing opportunity — specifically that publicly attributing US price competitiveness to the UK-US deal's preferential rate could itself attract USTR attention to the deal's architecture at the worst possible time ahead of the July 4 ratification deadline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must quantify the per-unit tariff cost on
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's FP&A team must, by 30 June 2026, quantify the per-unit Section 232 tariff cost on each of the estimated 1,000 above-cap vehicles shipped to the US in 2024 — calculated at 25% of US landed cost by nameplate — and incorporate the aggregate figure as a named baseline cost line 'US Above-Cap Tariff Exposure (FY2026 Carry)' within the FY2026 US budget, including both the retrospective 2024 overage cost and a forward projection for any FY2026 shipments that breach the 100,000-unit ceiling. The calculation must show the landed-cost basis by nameplate (differentiating at minimum Range Rover, Defender, and Discovery variants given their differing US MSRPs), be reviewed by the Group CFO, and be submitted to the Board Audit Committee as a separately identified item. Evidence of completion is the itemised tariff cost schedule signed by the CFO and its appearance as a named line in the FY2026 US budget document.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires quantification of above-cap tariff costs; this adaptation requires nameplate-level disaggregation because JLR's model mix determines the absolute tariff liability — a Range Rover above cap carries materially higher landed-cost exposure than a Discovery — and because the CFO sign-off anchors the figure as an auditable budget line rather than a planning estimate.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must stress-test its US EBIT contribution margin
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Group Finance function must, by 31 August 2026, produce a US EBIT stress-test model covering an FY2027 scenario in which total US shipment volume grows to 110,000 units, with the incremental 10,000 above-cap units bearing the full 25% Section 232 tariff at the prevailing GBP/USD exchange rate, and must calculate the resulting EBIT contribution margin compression relative to a 100,000-unit at-cap baseline. The model must identify the US EBIT break-even point — the per-unit price increase or volume reduction required to offset the 25% tariff on 10,000 incremental units — and must present at least two mitigation levers (e.g. nameplate reallocation to reduce below-cap volume, US local pricing adjustment, shipment timing management). The stress-test output must be reviewed by the Group CFO and presented to the Board's Finance Committee as a standalone agenda item, with the signed presentation constituting evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an EBIT stress test at 110,000 units; this adaptation anchors the test to JLR's confirmed 2024 shipment baseline of 101,000 units — meaning the 110,000-unit FY2027 scenario represents a realistic 9,000-unit organic growth trajectory, not a tail-risk scenario — and requires the model to identify specific mitigation levers rather than simply quantifying the exposure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR treasury must model FX and working-capital implications
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR Treasury must, by 31 July 2026, produce a working-capital and FX sensitivity model specifically for above-cap US shipments — defined as volumes exceeding the 100,000-unit annual threshold — that quantifies how GBP/USD rate movements of ±5% and ±10% from the rate prevailing on 1 May 2026 amplify or offset the 25% Section 232 landed-cost increase on those units, and identifies the GBP/USD rate at which the 25% tariff on above-cap vehicles becomes EBIT-neutral relative to sub-cap units. The model must also assess the timing mismatch between tariff payment (at US Customs entry) and revenue recognition (at US dealer delivery), and recommend whether JLR should adjust its above-cap shipment financing arrangements or FX hedge book to manage the cash-flow gap. The completed model, reviewed by the Group Treasurer and CFO, with a documented hedging recommendation, constitutes evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires FX and working-capital modelling on above-cap tariff timing differences; this adaptation specifies the GBP/USD break-even rate calculation as the critical analytical output — because JLR's functional currency is GBP while tariff liability is USD-denominated, making the exchange rate the primary variable determining whether the 25% above-cap exposure is a manageable cost line or a material EBIT drag.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must prepare a pre-cleared stakeholder communication line
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Communications Director, in coordination with the Government Affairs Director and Group General Counsel, must prepare and obtain CEO sign-off on a pre-cleared Q&A holding statement by 20 June 2026 — before the July 4, 2026 EU tariff ratification deadline generates heightened media scrutiny — that addresses the question of whether JLR is commercially benefiting from the tariff architecture that disadvantages Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Porsche, and Volkswagen on their EU-built US-bound vehicles. The holding statement must: accurately reflect confirmed facts (the May 2025 UK-US deal, the 10% rate on the first 100,000 units, the structural cap overage); acknowledge the above-cap exposure JLR itself faces on vehicles exceeding 100,000 units at the 25% Section 232 rate; avoid language that could be construed as endorsement of the tariff disadvantage imposed on EU competitors; and be pre-approved for use by UK government officials, investor relations, and media spokespeople without further escalation. Evidence of completion is the signed Q&A document, distribution confirmation to Communications, IR, and UK Government Affairs teams, and a dated CEO sign-off record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a pre-cleared stakeholder communication line; this adaptation specifies that the holding statement must proactively surface JLR's own above-cap 25% exposure — not only the competitive advantage — because a communication that acknowledges only the benefit without the structural cap risk would be factually misleading and would expose JLR to reputational and regulatory risk if the cap overage becomes public.

incidents.headline: 10 November 2026 — the calendar trigger no JLR EV launch can route around
incidents.summary: China MIIT publishes draft rare earth enforcement framework. October 2025 extraterritorial controls suspended — but only until 10 November 2026. IEA puts $3 trillion of automotive value at risk under full implementation. CSIS: US yttrium imports -95%. European dysprosium prices at 6× Chinese domestic. NdFeB magnet supply — needed for JLR EV traction motors — sits behind a Chinese export-licence decision. Suspension lapses 10 Nov 2026. After that date every magnet containing >0.1% Chinese-origin material requires a MOFCOM licence. Defence end-uses auto-rejected; civil licences case-by-case. Three of JLR's principal risk lines converge on a single calendar trigger — "Electrification Transition" (new in FY24/25), "Supply chain disruptions", and "Global economic and geopolitical environment" — all hit 10 November 2026 when China's MOFCOM heavy rare-earths suspension lapses. Every NdFeB traction-motor magnet for Range Rover Electric and the all-electric Jaguar GT routes through this single date. The register names the risks separately. The cards count them as one event. The pre-positioning decision — qualify a non-Chinese magnet source NOW, or wait — needs to be made before, not after, the trigger.

graded_claims.claim: China MIIT published a draft rare earth enforcement framework with extraterritorial controls suspended only until 10 November 2026
After 10 November 2026, every NdFeB magnet containing more than 0.1% Chinese-origin material requires a MOFCOM export licence
IEA estimates USD 3 trillion of automotive value is at risk under full implementation of China's rare earth export controls
CSIS reports US yttrium imports fell by approximately 95% following earlier Chinese export restriction actions
European dysprosium prices are at approximately 6x Chinese domestic prices
JLR's FY24/25 Annual Report names 'Electrification Transition', 'Supply chain disruptions', and 'Global economic and geopolitical environment' as distinct principal risks
Defence end-uses under the MOFCOM licence regime are auto-rejected; civil licences are issued case-by-case
NdFeB traction-motor magnets are required for Range Rover Electric and the all-electric Jaguar GT

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure JLR's NdFeB magnet supply chain for Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT is resilient to the lapse of China's MOFCOM rare earth export-licence suspension on 10 November 2026.
Monitor and respond to evolving MOFCOM and MIIT regulatory decisions on rare earth export licences affecting JLR's traction-motor magnet procurement.
Maintain situational awareness of the geopolitical dynamics driving China's extraterritorial rare earth controls and their direct intersection with JLR's electrification programme timeline.
Ensure JLR's principal risk disclosures for 'Electrification Transition' and 'Supply Chain Disruptions' are updated to reflect the 10 November 2026 MOFCOM suspension-lapse trigger.
Accelerate qualification of ex-China NdFeB magnet sources as a strategic prerequisite for JLR's Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT launch readiness.
Embed the 10 November 2026 MOFCOM lapse date as a hard planning constraint in JLR's electrification roadmap and strategic supplier contracting cycle.
Establish operational continuity procedures for JLR's traction-motor assembly operations in the event of a MOFCOM export-licence denial or delay affecting NdFeB magnet inbound supply.
Assess and remediate JLR's third-party magnet supplier exposure to Chinese-origin rare earth content above the 0.1% MOFCOM threshold triggering licence requirements.
Ensure ex-China NdFeB magnet qualification incorporates environmental and responsible sourcing standards aligned with JLR's sustainability commitments for Range Rover Electric.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must map all tier-1 and tier-2 NdFeB
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must commission, by 1 September 2026, a complete Chinese-origin content audit of every tier-1 and tier-2 NdFeB magnet supplier in the Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT traction-motor supply chains, mapping each supplier's alloy and sintering inputs against the reported 0.1% MOFCOM threshold; any supplier whose Chinese-origin content meets or exceeds that threshold must be escalated within five business days to the Electrification Programme Office for immediate qualification-review initiation. Evidence of completion comprises a signed audit report listing each supplier, their declared Chinese-origin content percentage, and the escalation log for any flagged supplier, all submitted to the Board Risk Committee no later than 30 September 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a mapping exercise; this adaptation specifies the accountable officer, the two named vehicle programmes, the alloy-and-sintering granularity required by the reported MOFCOM framework, the 1 September 2026 deadline that preserves reaction time before the 10 November 2026 lapse, and the escalation timeframe — none of which appear in the parent.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must maintain a real-time intelligence feed on
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: From 11 November 2026 onwards, JLR's Supply Chain Risk function must maintain a dedicated intelligence feed — drawing on at minimum MOFCOM public licence decision registers, specialist trade-intelligence services covering Chinese rare earth export administration, and UK DBT automotive sector advisories — that captures civil automotive NdFeB licence approvals, denials, and delays within 48 hours of publication and delivers a weekly digest to the Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT programme procurement leads. If the reported MOFCOM civil-licence case-by-case regime is confirmed operationally, any denial pattern affecting NdFeB sintered magnets for traction-motor applications must trigger an automatic procurement-pivot authority notice to the CPO within 24 hours, pre-authorising switch to contracted ex-China sources without further board approval.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires a real-time intelligence feed; this adaptation names the specific post-lapse date, the exact data sources and publication types to monitor, the 48-hour ingestion and weekly digest cadence, the two named programmes as recipients, and the pre-approved procurement-pivot trigger — converting a monitoring obligation into an actionable early-warning circuit.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must conduct a geopolitical scenario stress-test —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Electrification Programme Office, jointly with the Enterprise Risk function, must complete a formal geopolitical scenario stress-test by 31 July 2026 — before Q4 2026 start-of-production milestones are locked for Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT — modelling a binary MOFCOM licence-denial scenario in which zero Chinese-origin NdFeB traction-motor magnets are licensable from 10 November 2026; the stress-test must quantify production delay in weeks per vehicle programme, revenue exposure under JLR's published EV volume forecasts, and the minimum ex-China magnet volume required to sustain each programme's SOP schedule. The completed stress-test, including sensitivity tables and recommended mitigating actions, must be presented to and approved by the JLR Board Risk Committee no later than 15 August 2026, with acceptance of residual risk formally minuted.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires a geopolitical stress-test before Q4 SOP lock; this adaptation fixes the 31 July 2026 completion date, specifies the binary licence-denial scenario applied to both named vehicle programmes, defines the three quantitative outputs required, and establishes Board Risk Committee sign-off with minuted residual-risk acceptance — making the test a decision gate rather than an analytical exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish contractual dual-source obligations with at
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's CPO must execute, no later than 30 September 2026, binding dual-source supply agreements with at least one NdFeB magnet manufacturer whose entire production chain — from rare earth alloy to sintered magnet — operates outside Chinese jurisdiction and therefore requires no MOFCOM export licence; the agreements must specify minimum annual volume commitments sufficient to cover at least 50% of combined Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT traction-motor magnet demand in the first twelve months post-SOP, include a step-up clause activatable within 60 days if the primary Chinese-origin source is licence-blocked from 10 November 2026, and be countersigned by the supplier's authorised representative. Evidence of completion is the fully executed contract, lodged with JLR Legal and reported to the Board Risk Committee by 15 October 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires contractual dual-source obligations with an ex-China supplier; this adaptation sets the 30 September 2026 execution deadline, specifies the no-MOFCOM-licence eligibility criterion, anchors volume commitments to the two named programmes' combined demand, defines a 60-day step-up activation window keyed to the 10 November 2026 lapse, and requires Board Risk Committee reporting — converting a contractual obligation into a quantified, time-bound, verified commitment.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's crisis response playbook must include a pre-approved
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's General Counsel, in coordination with the Investor Relations and Communications functions, must develop and obtain board pre-approval — no later than 31 October 2026 — of a parent-company disclosure protocol addressed to Tata Motors Limited that is triggered if MOFCOM licence denial materially delays Range Rover Electric or Jaguar GT start-of-production; the protocol must define 'material delay' as any SOP slippage exceeding 30 days attributable to NdFeB magnet supply disruption, specify the notification timeline (within 5 business days of trigger), identify the JLR executive responsible for initiating the disclosure, and include draft disclosure language compliant with Tata Motors' listing obligations on BSE and NSE. The approved protocol must be stored in JLR's crisis management system and rehearsed in the Q3 2026 crisis simulation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires a pre-approved Tata Motors disclosure protocol for MOFCOM-denial delays; this adaptation names both vehicle programmes, defines 'material delay' quantitatively, sets the 30-day slippage threshold, specifies Tata Motors' BSE/NSE listing-compliance requirement, establishes the 31 October 2026 approval deadline, and mandates a Q3 2026 crisis-simulation rehearsal — grounding a communications obligation in executable legal and governance specifics.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must monitor MIIT and MOFCOM regulatory publications
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supply Chain Risk function must establish, by 1 August 2026, a structured monitoring protocol that scans MIIT and MOFCOM official publications — including the MIIT rare earth enforcement framework consultation register, MOFCOM export-control notices, and State Council rare earth administrative regulations — no less than weekly, and classifies any amendment, extension, or tightening of the October 2025 extraterritorial controls suspension as a Category 1 regulatory change requiring board notification within 48 hours of detection. The monitoring protocol must name a designated analyst accountable for each weekly scan cycle, maintain a dated evidence log of all publications reviewed, and route any Category 1 change simultaneously to the JLR CEO, CPO, and Chair of the Board Risk Committee — with the notification record preserved as an audit trail confirming the board was informed before 10 November 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires monitoring for amendments and board notification; this adaptation names the specific MIIT and MOFCOM publication channels to watch, sets a weekly scan frequency with a designated accountable analyst, defines a Category 1 classification for suspension changes, specifies the 48-hour notification window and the three named recipients, and mandates a dated audit log — translating a monitoring obligation into a documented, accountable, auditable process.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must engage UK Department for Business and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Director of Government Affairs must initiate formal engagement with the UK Department for Business and Trade's Automotive Transformation Fund (ATF) programme team by 30 September 2026, submitting a written representation that requests ATF conditionality and supply-chain resilience criteria explicitly account for MOFCOM suspension-lapse risk in JLR's EV programme; the representation must reference the 10 November 2026 lapse date, the NdFeB magnet dependency of Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT, and request DBT guidance on whether ATF funding conditionality can be structured to support ex-China magnet supplier qualification costs. Evidence of completion comprises the dated written submission, any DBT acknowledgement received, and a summary of outcomes reported to the JLR Board by 31 October 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires DBT engagement on ATF conditionality; this adaptation specifies the Director of Government Affairs as accountable, sets the 30 September 2026 engagement deadline, names the two vehicle programmes and the 10 November 2026 trigger, defines the specific ask (conditionality for ex-China qualification costs), and requires a Board-reported outcome summary — converting a lobbying intention into a documented government engagement with defined deliverables.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's Electrification Programme Office must treat the 10
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Electrification Programme Office Director must present to the JLR Board, no later than 30 September 2026 (Q3 2026), a board-approved ex-China NdFeB magnet qualification plan that treats 10 November 2026 as a non-negotiable strategic forcing function; the plan must cover both Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT traction-motor programmes, specify at minimum one fully qualified ex-China supplier with confirmed production capacity, state the volume of magnet inventory to be secured before 10 November 2026, identify any engineering or performance gaps relative to current Chinese-origin specifications, and carry a signed board resolution confirming approval or escalation of residual risk. Absence of a board-approved plan by 30 September 2026 must be reported immediately to the JLR Audit Committee as an unmitigated critical risk.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires a board-approved qualification plan by Q3 2026; this adaptation names the Electrification Programme Office Director as accountable, mandates coverage of both vehicle programmes, specifies the content requirements (supplier identity, capacity confirmation, inventory target, engineering gap analysis), requires a signed board resolution, and imposes an Audit Committee escalation if the deadline is missed — making the Q3 deadline a hard governance gate rather than a planning aspiration.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must evaluate whether the 0
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Engineer for Electrification must commission, by 31 August 2026, an engineering review assessing whether the reported 0.1% Chinese-origin content threshold under the MOFCOM framework necessitates changes to the NdFeB magnet alloy compositions, sintering processes, or dimensional tolerances currently specified for Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT traction motors; the review must evaluate each available ex-China magnet source against JLR's current motor performance specifications (torque density, demagnetisation resistance, thermal tolerance) and identify any specification changes required to accept ex-China magnets without motor redesign. The review findings, including a recommended specification-change decision and estimated engineering lead time, must be submitted to the Electrification Programme Office and the Board Risk Committee by 30 September 2026 to inform the Q3 board qualification-plan approval.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires evaluation of whether the MOFCOM threshold triggers magnet specification redesign; this adaptation names the Chief Engineer for Electrification as accountable, specifies the three technical parameters to be evaluated (torque density, demagnetisation resistance, thermal tolerance) against real ex-China sources, sets the 31 August 2026 commission date and 30 September 2026 submission date aligned to the Q3 board gate, and anchors the review explicitly in both vehicle programmes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must develop a traction-motor assembly continuity plan
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP of Manufacturing Operations and VP of Supply Chain must jointly develop, and submit for Board Risk Committee approval by 15 October 2026, a traction-motor assembly continuity plan that sustains Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT production through a minimum 90-day NdFeB magnet supply interruption commencing on 10 November 2026; the plan must specify the safety-stock build target in magnet units per vehicle programme to be physically warehoused at JLR's Solihull and Castle Bromwich facilities before 10 November 2026, the storage, handling, and inventory-management protocols for sintered NdFeB magnets, the production sequencing rules that prioritise highest-margin variants during constrained supply, and the supplier-switching decision tree activated if MOFCOM licence denial is confirmed. Evidence of execution is a physical stock count report confirming safety-stock targets are met no later than 7 November 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires a 90-day continuity plan with safety-stock targets before 10 November 2026; this adaptation names both accountable VPs and both vehicle programmes, anchors the 90-day bridge to the specific lapse date, specifies Solihull and Castle Bromwich as the storage locations, requires a production-sequencing rule and supplier-switching decision tree, and mandates a physical stock count report by 7 November 2026 as the definitive evidence of completion.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must contractually require all tier-1 magnet suppliers
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's CPO must ensure that, by 31 October 2026, all tier-1 NdFeB magnet supply contracts for Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT traction-motor programmes are amended to include: (a) a mandatory disclosure obligation requiring the supplier to declare Chinese-origin content percentages at both the rare earth alloy sourcing stage and the sintered magnet stage, with declarations submitted to JLR quarterly and within 10 business days of any change; (b) a right-to-audit clause exercisable by JLR at 30 days' notice if declared content at either stage reaches or exceeds 0.05% — the pre-trigger monitoring threshold — or if JLR's intelligence feed identifies divergence between declared and market-reported content levels. Non-execution of amended contracts by 31 October 2026 must be reported to the Board Risk Committee as a critical third-party risk gap.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires disclosure obligations and a right-to-audit clause; this adaptation specifies the two-stage disclosure granularity (alloy sourcing and sintered magnet), the quarterly and change-event cadence, the 0.05% pre-trigger audit threshold distinct from the reported 0.1% MOFCOM trigger, the 30-day audit notice period, the 31 October 2026 contract-amendment deadline, and the Board Risk Committee escalation for non-execution — making an abstract contractual requirement operationally precise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must update its third-party supplier risk assessments
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supplier Risk Management must update the third-party risk assessment records for all NdFeB magnet suppliers in the Range Rover Electric and Jaguar GT supply chains by 30 September 2026, reclassifying any supplier whose Chinese-origin alloy content exceeds 0.1% as a Critical Single-Point-of-Failure (CSPF) risk; CSPF classification must automatically trigger a Board Risk Committee notification within five business days, initiate an accelerated alternative-source qualification under the Electrification Programme Office's ex-China plan, and prohibit any new volume commitment to that supplier beyond existing contractual minimums until an ex-China alternative is qualified. The updated risk assessments and any resulting CSPF notifications must be presented at the Board Risk Committee's next scheduled meeting following the 30 September 2026 deadline.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires updating supplier risk assessments to flag suppliers above 0.1% Chinese-origin content; this adaptation names the Head of Supplier Risk Management as accountable, defines the CSPF classification label, specifies the five-day Board Risk Committee notification window, adds a volume-commitment freeze and alternative-source acceleration as automatic consequences of CSPF status, and ties the reporting cycle to the Board Risk Committee's actual meeting schedule — converting a classification update into a governance-linked risk-management action.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must validate that any ex-China NdFeB magnet
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Responsible Sourcing must conduct, in parallel with ex-China NdFeB magnet supplier qualification activities and no later than 30 days after any candidate supplier is identified under the MOFCOM-avoidance strategy, a formal responsible sourcing assessment of that supplier against JLR's Responsible Sourcing Standard for rare earth minerals as published in JLR's FY24/25 sustainability report; the assessment must evaluate the candidate's rare earth mining provenance, labour practices, environmental permits, and chain-of-custody documentation, and must produce a pass/refer/fail determination before that supplier can be approved for Range Rover Electric or Jaguar GT traction-motor magnet supply. Any supplier receiving a 'refer' or 'fail' determination must be reported to the ESG Committee alongside the procurement rationale if the CPO nonetheless proposes proceeding, ensuring no MOFCOM-avoidance commercial pressure overrides JLR's published sustainability commitments.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The parent requires responsible sourcing validation for ex-China suppliers; this adaptation ties the assessment to the actual qualification timeline (30 days post-candidate identification), specifies the four evaluation dimensions drawn from JLR's own FY24/25 standard, creates a three-outcome determination gate that blocks supply approval, and introduces an ESG Committee escalation path if commercial MOFCOM-avoidance pressure conflicts with a negative finding — preventing the supply-chain emergency from bypassing JLR's sustainability commitments.

incidents.headline: Solihull dark for thirteen days — the Norwegian supplier no one will name
incidents.summary: On 26 March 2026 JLR informed its supply base of a production pause at the Solihull plant covering Range Rover and Range Rover Sport assembly until 8 April. Root cause: a fire at a key parts supplier in Norway. Industry inference: Neuman Aluminium Raufoss (publicly named JLR Tier-1 for aluminium chassis components). Neither party has confirmed. Output of two of JLR's most profitable nameplates suspended for 13 days. Plant workers continued to report to Solihull during the shutdown — wage cost preserved, output not. JLR cited a "part supply challenge" to the Financial Times. Customer order book reportedly absorbed the disruption given existing multi-month waitlists. This is the second material Solihull halt in seven months: first cyber (Sep 2025), now physical (Mar 2026). Both single-supplier failures, both 13 days, both on Range Rover. JLR's FY24/25 report claims the Kass / Secure26 programme has "industrialised risk management". The empirical evidence this quarter says otherwise: one Norwegian supplier fire took 13 days out of two of JLR's three most-profitable nameplates. The register treats "Supply chain disruptions" as a single line — the cards count it as repeat-event from a different failure mode each time, which means the control needs to be on concentration, not on the type of disruption.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: RANGE ROVER REVENUE AT RISK (13 DAYS)

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that single-source supplier dependencies for critical production components — specifically sole-source aluminium chassis-component Tier-1s supplying the Solihull Range Rover lines — are identified, stress-tested, and subject to documented concentration-limit controls.

Maintain operational continuity for the Solihull plant's highest-margin assembly lines (Range Rover and Range Rover Sport) against physical supply-disruption scenarios, including fire at a single-site overseas supplier.

Ensure business-continuity plans for Solihull production are tested against physical failure modes (not only cyber), given that both the September 2025 cyber event and the March 2026 fire each independently halted output for 13 days.

Ensure that third-party concentration risk — specifically single-site, single-country Tier-1 supplier reliance for Range Rover aluminium chassis components — is explicitly monitored and escalated as a board-level risk rather than subsumed under a generic 'supply chain disruptions' register line.

Complete documented due diligence on all Tier-1 suppliers providing sole-sourced structural components to the Solihull plant, with particular focus on physical-site resilience (fire suppression, business continuity, alternative manufacturing capacity) at Norwegian and other single-country facilities.

Ensure physical risk at critical supplier sites — including fire risk at the inferred Neuman Aluminium Raufoss facility — is assessed and contractually required to meet defined resilience standards as a condition of Tier-1 sole-source status.

Ensure JLR's external communications during a production halt are sufficiently transparent to protect brand credibility, given that vague language ('part supply challenge') during the March 2026 event left industry and media to name the supplier and failure mode independently.
