adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must conduct a formal supplier-concentration review identifying
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 June 2026, JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must deliver to the Tata Motors board a formal supplier-concentration report that maps every sole-sourced Tier-1 component feeding the Solihull Range Rover and Range Rover Sport assembly lines, quantifies the maximum consecutive production-halt exposure per supplier (expressed in days and estimated revenue loss), and documents board-approved exposure limits above which mandatory dual-sourcing or buffer-stock investment is triggered. The report must explicitly flag the aluminium chassis-component category implicated in the 26 March–8 April 2026 halt as a Category-1 concentration risk, irrespective of whether the inferred Norwegian supplier is formally named, and must include a remediation roadmap with milestone dates. Board sign-off, evidenced by board-minute extract, is required before the report is considered closed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a concentration review; this adaptation anchors it to the specific Solihull lines, the specific 13-day halt duration as the calibration benchmark for exposure limits, and requires the aluminium chassis category to be pre-flagged rather than discovered through the review process.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish or validate a dual-source or
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 90 days of 8 April 2026, JLR's Head of Supply Chain must present to the Chief Procurement Officer a validated dual-source or bonded-buffer-stock plan for aluminium chassis components supplied to the Solihull Range Rover and Range Rover Sport lines from the sole-source Norwegian facility implicated in the March 2026 fire event, targeting a minimum 15 operational-day buffer measured against confirmed daily consumption rates at Solihull. The plan must identify at least one qualified alternative source or bonded inventory site, state the capital and lead-time requirements to achieve the target buffer, and include a go/no-go decision gate with named executive accountable for authorising investment; if the 15-day buffer cannot be achieved within 12 months, the residual gap must be escalated to the Tata Motors board as an unmitigated critical risk. Evidence of completion is a signed plan document, a procurement or inventory commitment, and a board or executive-committee record confirming the decision.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control sets the 15-day buffer target; this adaptation calibrates it explicitly against the 13-day halt already experienced, names the Norwegian aluminium category as the immediate subject, and adds an escalation obligation when the target cannot be met within the defined timeline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's Kass/Secure26 programme must be updated to include
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 31 May 2026, the JLR risk function, acting through the Kass/Secure26 programme, must revise the Solihull plant's operational risk register to disaggregate the current single 'supply chain disruptions' entry into at minimum three distinct risk categories — (i) physical-site failure (fire, flood, structural loss) at a Tier-1 supplier, (ii) cyber or systems failure at a Tier-1 supplier or JLR itself, and (iii) logistical or capacity disruption — each with its own likelihood rating, impact rating, control owner, and treatment plan. The revision must record the September 2025 cyber halt and the March 2026 Norwegian-supplier fire halt as separate historical loss events in the register, preventing future aggregation that obscures repeat-event patterns on the Range Rover and Range Rover Sport lines. Completion evidence is the updated register approved by the Kass/Secure26 programme board and filed with the JLR Audit Committee.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires physical and cyber modes to be separated; this adaptation requires the two actual Solihull halts — one cyber, one physical, both 13 days, both sole-supplier — to be recorded as discrete historical events so the register cannot mask concentration-of-failure-mode patterns going forward.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must stress-test its Solihull production-continuity plan against
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Commencing with the quarter ending 30 June 2026, JLR's Chief Financial Officer and Chief Operations Officer must jointly submit to the Tata Motors board a production-continuity stress-test result for the Solihull plant, modelling the financial exposure — in lost Range Rover and Range Rover Sport unit output, preserved but unproductive wage cost, and gross margin impact — of a sole-source Tier-1 aluminium chassis supplier absence for 7, 13, and 21 consecutive days, using the confirmed 26 March–8 April 2026 event as the baseline calibration scenario. The stress-test must document the point at which fixed labour cost (workers reporting to Solihull with no output, as confirmed during the March 2026 halt) converts from a short-term absorption to a board-level financial risk, and must include the mitigation actions required to reduce the 21-day exposure below an agreed board threshold. This submission becomes a standing quarterly board agenda item until the identified mitigations are fully implemented.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires stress-testing against up to 21 days; this adaptation uses the confirmed 13-day halt and the confirmed preserved-wage-cost dynamic as the empirical calibration point, and frames the stress-test as a recurring board deliverable rather than a one-time exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must implement real-time physical-site monitoring (including fire-event
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 September 2026, JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must implement a real-time physical-site monitoring capability — covering as a minimum fire-alarm integration, utilities-loss detection, and site-access anomaly alerting — at every sole-sourced Tier-1 facility supplying chassis or structural components to the Solihull Range Rover and Range Rover Sport lines, with contractual obligations on those suppliers to connect their facility-management or fire-suppression systems to a JLR-accessible alerting channel. The system must be configured so that a confirmed fire or site-loss event at any covered facility triggers an internal JLR escalation to the Head of Supply Chain and Chief Procurement Officer within four hours of detection, replacing the notification sequence evidenced in the March 2026 event where JLR informed its supply base on 26 March — the same day production was paused — without publicly disclosing when the fire actually occurred. Completion evidence is a live technical integration certificate for each covered facility and a tested escalation drill result on file.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires real-time monitoring; this adaptation anchors the four-hour escalation target to the confirmed gap between the fire occurrence and JLR's 26 March supply-base notification, and specifies contractual connectivity obligations on suppliers as the mechanism.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must define and test a formal production-restart
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 31 July 2026, JLR's Chief Operations Officer must publish and table-top test a formal Production-Restart Protocol for the Solihull plant, designed to activate within 24 hours of confirmed sole-source Tier-1 supplier failure, covering: (i) an emergency sourcing decision tree for aluminium chassis components including pre-qualified alternative suppliers and bonded-stock drawdown authority; (ii) a customer-communication sequence defining who issues statements, in what channel, and within what timeframe — correcting the March 2026 approach of publicly characterising a 13-day production halt only as a 'part supply challenge' with no supplier identification; and (iii) a workforce-redeployment plan that specifies productive activities for Solihull plant workers during a halt rather than a default continued-attendance model that preserves wage cost without output. The protocol must be tested via a live tabletop exercise against the March 2026 scenario, with findings and remediation actions documented and signed off by the COO before the protocol is declared operational.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a restart protocol; this adaptation is built directly around the three specific deficiencies evidenced in the March 2026 event — absence of emergency sourcing, a minimal public disclosure, and no evidenced workforce redeployment — turning each gap into a named protocol component.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must update its third-party risk register to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 June 2026, JLR's third-party risk function must reclassify all Tier-1 suppliers providing aluminium chassis or structural components to the Solihull Range Rover and Range Rover Sport lines as 'Critical Sole-Source' in JLR's third-party risk register, and must issue to each such supplier — including the Norwegian facility implicated in the March 2026 fire, if and when formally identified — a contractual addendum requiring annual physical-resilience attestation covering: confirmed fire-suppression system certification to a named standard, a tested and dated business continuity plan demonstrating alternative production-site capacity, and a signed declaration of maximum output recovery time following a total site-loss event. The first attestation cycle must be completed and results filed with the JLR Audit Committee no later than 31 December 2026. Completion evidence is the updated risk-register classification, the executed contractual addenda, and the filed attestation returns.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires critical-supplier classification and attestation; this adaptation specifies aluminium chassis components for Solihull as the immediate population, ties the attestation content directly to the fire-suppression and BCP gaps exposed by the March 2026 event, and sets a concrete first-cycle deadline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must contractually require the sole-source Norwegian aluminium
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 31 July 2026, JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must ensure that all contracts with sole-sourced Tier-1 suppliers of chassis or structural components for the Solihull Range Rover and Range Rover Sport lines — including the Norwegian aluminium supplier implicated in the March 2026 fire — are amended to include a mandatory material-damage disclosure obligation requiring the supplier to notify JLR's Head of Supply Chain within 24 hours of any event causing or likely to cause loss of production capacity at the supplying facility, with 'material damage' defined to include fire, flood, structural failure, or any other event affecting more than 10% of the facility's capacity for more than 48 hours. The amendment must specify financial or contractual consequences for non-disclosure and must grant JLR audit rights to verify facility status independently. Completion evidence is executed contract amendments and a register entry confirming the 24-hour disclosure obligation is live for each covered supplier.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires 24-hour disclosure; this adaptation defines 'material damage' with a quantitative production-capacity threshold directly derived from the March 2026 event and adds audit rights and non-disclosure consequences as enforcement mechanisms absent from the generic requirement.

incidents.headline: Anjun Industrial — the Tier-1 fire that levelled 70 million units of capacity overnight
incidents.summary: A fire at the Anjun Industrial engine-valve plant in Daedeok district, Daejeon, killed 14 workers and injured ~60 on 20 March 2026. Five individuals including the CEO were booked under occupational-negligence charges; the Serious Accidents Punishment Act is now being applied. The plant produced ~70 million engine valves annually before being destroyed. Anjun was a first-tier supplier to Hyundai Motor and Kia with substantial export exposure (US subsidiary since 2009). Hyundai and Kia will need existing inventory until an alternative supplier is qualified — typically 6–18 months for safety-critical engine components. Korea Transnational Corporations Watch Network issued a formal due-diligence query to Hyundai and Kia on 21 April 2026. The read-across is structural, not anecdotal. Five Korean Tier-1 executives are now criminally charged after a fire — establishing that CSDDD-type personal liability is enforceable in Asia, not just on paper in Europe. JLR's supplier base includes a long tail of South Korean and broader Asian Tier-1/2 suppliers where fire-life-safety is materially under-controlled vs European norms. The Daejeon precedent both raises the legal floor and signals where the next analogous event is most likely to come from — and JLR's supplier audit programme is not yet structured around physical-failure concentration at the Tier-1+ layer.

graded_claims.claim: A fire at the Anjun Industrial engine-valve plant in Daedeok district, Daejeon, South Korea occurred on 20 March 2026.
The fire killed 14 workers and injured approximately 60.
Five individuals including the CEO were booked under occupational-negligence charges; the Serious Accidents Punishment Act is being applied.
Anjun produced approximately 70 million engine valves annually at Plant No. 1 before the fire destroyed it.
Anjun was a first-tier supplier to Hyundai Motor and Kia and is one of only two engine-valve manufacturers in South Korea.
Hyundai Motor began experiencing full-scale production setbacks across major models including the Kona and Genesis G90 from early April 2026.
Partial production normalization was not expected until June 2026.
Korea Transnational Corporations Watch Network issued a formal due-diligence query to Hyundai and Kia on 21 April 2026.
Anjun established a US local subsidiary in 2009.
About 200 kilograms of sodium were stored onsite, complicating firefighting operations.
The Aricell battery-plant CEO was sentenced to 15 years in prison following the 2024 Hwaseong fire that killed 23 workers.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that JLR's supplier physical-risk programme identifies and mitigates single-point-of-failure concentration at Tier-1 manufacturing sites, including fire-life-safety standards at South Korean and Asian suppliers where the Anjun/Daejeon pattern is replicable.

Ensure that JLR's third-party due-diligence programme captures fire-life-safety and occupational-safety conditions at Tier-1 suppliers in jurisdictions where the Serious Accidents Punishment Act or equivalent criminal liability has been activated.

Ensure that JLR maintains documented supplier qualification criteria for safety-critical engine components that include physical-site risk assessment, given the 6–18 month requalification window established by the Anjun incident.

Ensure that JLR's contractual supplier-conduct requirements extend to fire-life-safety and workplace-safety obligations enforceable through the same framework now applied by MOEL under South Korea's Serious Accidents Punishment Act.

Ensure that JLR monitors occupational-safety obligations and criminal liability exposure across its Tier-1 supply chain, following the Daejeon precedent establishing personal criminal liability for five Anjun executives under SAPA.

Ensure that JLR's operational continuity planning accounts for single-source Tier-1 capacity destruction events, modelled on the Anjun scenario of overnight loss of 70 million units of annual engine-valve capacity.

Ensure that JLR monitors regulatory and legislative developments in key supplier jurisdictions — specifically South Korea's SAPA enforcement trajectory — that may establish precedent for extraterritorial supply-chain liability applicable to JLR as a downstream OEM customer.

source_anchor.citation: Seoul Economic Daily, 'Engine Valve Shortage Halts Hyundai, Kia Production in Domino Effect', 3 April 2026

clean_claims.claim: A fire at the Anjun Industrial engine-valve plant in Daedeok district, Daejeon, South Korea occurred on 20 March 2026.
The fire killed 14 workers and injured approximately 60.
Five individuals including the CEO were booked under occupational-negligence charges; the Serious Accidents Punishment Act is being applied.
Anjun produced approximately 70 million engine valves annually at Plant No. 1 before the fire destroyed it.
Anjun was a first-tier supplier to Hyundai Motor and Kia and is one of only two engine-valve manufacturers in South Korea.
Hyundai Motor began experiencing full-scale production setbacks across major models including the Kona and Genesis G90 from early April 2026.
Korea Transnational Corporations Watch Network issued a formal due-diligence query to Hyundai and Kia on 21 April 2026.
About 200 kilograms of sodium were stored onsite, complicating firefighting operations.
The Aricell battery-plant CEO was sentenced to 15 years in prison following the 2024 Hwaseong fire that killed 23 workers.

held_claims.claim: Partial production normalization was not expected until June 2026.
Anjun established a US local subsidiary in 2009.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must conduct physical fire-life-safety audits of all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supplier Quality must commission physical fire-life-safety audits of all South Korean and broader Asian Tier-1 single-source suppliers within 90 days of 20 March 2026, using the Anjun Industrial Daedeok district fire — where approximately 200 kilograms of onsite sodium complicated active firefighting and contributed to total plant destruction — as the reference hazard scenario for audit scope and checklist design. Each audit must specifically assess: reactive-metal or hazardous-material storage volumes and segregation, sprinkler and suppression system adequacy against chemical-accelerated fire, egress compliance, and single-building production concentration risk. Completion evidence is a signed audit report per supplier, a risk-rated findings register, and a corrective-action plan with closure dates submitted to JLR Supplier Risk by day 90.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The incident-specific contextualisation names the sodium-accelerated fire pathway at Anjun as the reference scenario, preventing a generic checklist that would miss reactive-metal storage as a key audit criterion at Korean and Asian manufacturing sites.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must map all safety-critical engine components —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Purchasing and Supplier Risk functions must jointly complete a concentration-risk mapping exercise for all safety-critical engine components — explicitly including valvetrain and engine-valve series — against Tier-1 single-site supply volume by 30 June 2026, flagging any supplier where a single manufacturing plant accounts for more than 50% of JLR's annual requirement for that part number. The mapping must reference the Anjun Plant No. 1 destruction scenario, in which a reported ~70 million-valve annual capacity was eliminated overnight at a facility confirmed to be one of only two engine-valve manufacturers in South Korea, and must record for each flagged supplier: the at-risk annual volume, the next-best qualified source, and the estimated requalification lead time. The completed map must be reviewed and signed off by JLR's Chief Procurement Officer and retained as a living register updated at each annual supplier review.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Anchoring the 50% single-site threshold directly to the Anjun overnight-destruction fact pattern prevents the mapping from being treated as a theoretical exercise and forces JLR to quantify actual valvetrain exposure against a confirmed total-loss event.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must update its business-continuity playbook to include
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Business Continuity and Supplier Risk teams must update the corporate supplier business-continuity playbook by the next annual supplier review (and no later than 20 September 2026) to include a named scenario — 'Tier-1 Total Plant Destruction: Engine-Critical Components' — modelled on the overnight loss of Anjun Industrial Plant No. 1 and the confirmed production setbacks experienced by Hyundai across models including the Kona and Genesis G90 from early April 2026. The updated playbook must specify: pre-qualified alternative sources for each engine-critical component category, maximum tolerable gap periods before line stoppage, and activation triggers and accountable roles for emergency resourcing. No component category may remain in the playbook without at least one pre-qualified alternative source identified; gaps must be escalated to the CPO as an open risk item until resolved.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Naming the Anjun overnight-destruction scenario explicitly in the playbook forces JLR to move beyond generic disruption templates and stress-test continuity plans against the confirmed failure mode of sudden, complete loss of a Tier-1 single-site engine-component facility.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must verify that all South Korean Tier-1
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Quality function must, within 60 days of 20 March 2026, obtain documentary evidence that all South Korean Tier-1 suppliers hold current fire-safety certificates compliant with Korean National Fire Agency standards, with specific verification that certificates remain valid and have not been suspended or placed under review in the context of the Ministry of Employment and Labour's post-Daejeon nationwide emergency inspection programme triggered by the confirmed Anjun fire and the associated criminal charges brought under the Serious Accidents Punishment Act. Where a supplier cannot provide a current, unrestricted certificate within the 60-day window, JLR's Supplier Quality Director must escalate the supplier to 'elevated-risk' status and initiate a physical verification visit within a further 30 days. Evidence of completion is a compliance matrix listing each South Korean Tier-1 supplier, certificate reference, expiry date, and any MOEL inspection status, held on the JLR supplier risk system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Connecting the certificate-verification requirement to the confirmed SAPA prosecutions and the post-Daejeon MOEL emergency inspection directive gives the control a live regulatory trigger, not merely a periodic audit obligation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must formally extend its supplier code of
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Legal and Procurement functions must, within 60 days of 20 March 2026, issue a formal amendment to the supplier code of conduct applicable to all South Korean and comparable Asian-jurisdiction Tier-1 suppliers, requiring those suppliers to notify JLR in writing within 30 days of any individual — including the CEO or equivalent officer — being booked or charged under the Korean Serious Accidents Punishment Act or a local criminal equivalent, as occurred when five Anjun Industrial individuals including the CEO were charged following the 20 March 2026 Daejeon fire. The amendment must specify that non-disclosure is a material breach of the supplier relationship entitling JLR to audit, remediation demand, or contract suspension. Receipt of the signed amendment from each in-scope supplier must be logged on the JLR supplier risk register and reported to the CPO within 90 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Naming the SAPA and the Anjun CEO-charge precedent makes the disclosure obligation concrete and jurisdiction-specific, preventing the code-of-conduct amendment from being drafted as a generic 'legal proceedings' clause that suppliers can interpret narrowly.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must require all South Korean and Asian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Responsible Business and Procurement functions must, within 45 days of 20 March 2026, distribute a human rights and worker-safety due-diligence questionnaire to all South Korean and Asian Tier-1 suppliers, structured to address the same categories raised in the Korea Transnational Corporations Watch Network's formal query issued to Hyundai and Kia on 21 April 2026 — specifically covering: fire-life-safety compliance, hazardous-material handling and storage controls (with reference to reactive-metal storage of the type confirmed at the Anjun site), worker injury and fatality reporting, and Serious Accidents Punishment Act compliance status. Completed questionnaires must be returned within 30 days of distribution; non-response or materially incomplete responses must be escalated by JLR's Head of Responsible Sourcing to the CPO and treated as a supplier-risk flag requiring follow-up audit.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Aligning the questionnaire scope explicitly with the Korea TNC Watch Network query categories — including reactive-metal storage — ensures JLR's due-diligence posture addresses the confirmed failure modes at Anjun rather than a generic ESG checklist.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must identify whether any current Tier-1 or
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Risk function must, within 45 days of 20 March 2026, conduct a structured review of all current South Korean Tier-1 and Tier-2 suppliers to identify any that operate the Anjun single-site production model for safety-critical engine components — specifically suppliers where a single plant accounts for a dominant share of output and where JLR has no pre-qualified alternative — documenting for each: the component category, annual volume at risk, whether the supplier is one of a limited number of qualified producers in-country (as Anjun was reported to be one of only two engine-valve manufacturers in South Korea), and the estimated requalification lead time for an alternative source given the confirmed 6–18 month certification window for safety-critical engine components. The completed register must be reviewed by the CPO and used to prioritise alternative-source qualification actions under AC-OPS-030-FIR-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Requiring the register to flag suppliers matching the 'limited in-country producer' characteristic of Anjun focuses the identification exercise on the structural concentration risk confirmed by this incident, not merely generic single-site dependency.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must insert a supplier-conduct clause requiring compliance
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Legal and Procurement functions must insert a supplier-conduct clause into all South Korean Tier-1 supplier contracts at next scheduled renewal or within 12 months of 20 March 2026 — whichever is sooner — requiring the supplier to: maintain compliance with the Korean Serious Accidents Punishment Act and its implementing regulations, promptly notify JLR of any SAPA investigation or charge (as occurred when five Anjun Industrial individuals including the CEO were charged following the 20 March 2026 fire), and remediate any MOEL-identified fire-safety or occupational-safety deficiency within timelines set by the regulator or JLR, whichever is shorter. The clause must stipulate that a conviction or unresolved material SAPA charge is grounds for JLR to invoke force-majeure or termination-for-cause provisions. Procurement's contract management system must be updated to track clause insertion status and renewal dates for all in-scope South Korean Tier-1 contracts.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Referencing SAPA by name and tying the clause trigger to the confirmed Anjun criminal-charge event makes this a jurisdiction-specific liability control rather than a generic code-of-conduct reference, reflecting the confirmed enforceability of personal criminal liability in Korea.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish a minimum 90-day strategic inventory
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supply Chain Operations function must, within 60 days of 20 March 2026, establish and maintain a minimum 90-day strategic inventory buffer for all engine-critical single-source components — including any engine-valve series equivalent to those produced at Anjun Industrial Plant No. 1 — where the qualified supplier base has fewer than two active manufacturing sites capable of fulfilling JLR's requirement. Buffer sizing must be calculated against the confirmed overnight-destruction scenario (total loss of a single Tier-1 plant in one event, as occurred at Anjun on 20 March 2026) and must account for the confirmed 6–18 month requalification lead time for safety-critical engine components, not a shorter generic disruption window. The buffer policy must be approved by JLR's VP Supply Chain, recorded in the inventory risk register, and reviewed quarterly until at least one pre-qualified alternative source is active for each covered component.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Sizing the buffer against the confirmed 6–18 month requalification window and the overnight total-loss scenario prevents the inventory target from being set against a shorter, generic disruption assumption that would leave JLR exposed in an Anjun-equivalent event.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must initiate parallel qualification of at least
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Purchasing and Supplier Quality functions must, within 30 days of 20 March 2026, identify every engine-valve series and safety-critical valvetrain component currently sourced from a Korean single-site Tier-1 supplier, and immediately initiate parallel qualification of at least one non-Korean alternative supplier for each such series, targeting completion of qualification within the confirmed 6–18 month certification window applicable to safety-critical engine components. Qualification programmes must be formally resourced with dedicated supplier quality engineers, include full technical review and dyno/validation testing schedules, and have milestone checkpoints at 90, 180, and 365 days reported to the CPO. Where the reported status of Anjun as one of only two South Korean engine-valve manufacturers constrains in-country options, JLR must actively canvass qualified manufacturers outside Korea, including European, Japanese, and North American sources.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Constraining the alternative-source search to non-Korean suppliers where in-country options are structurally limited directly addresses the confirmed market-concentration risk exposed by Anjun's status as one of only two South Korean engine-valve producers.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR's legal function must assess whether the Korea
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's General Counsel must commission a written legal assessment, completed within 30 days of 20 March 2026, evaluating whether the Korea Transnational Corporations Watch Network's formal due-diligence query issued to Hyundai and Kia on 21 April 2026 — referencing the Anjun fire, the confirmed deaths of 14 workers, and the criminal charges under the Serious Accidents Punishment Act — creates analogous supplier-oversight reporting obligations for JLR under the UK Modern Slavery Act 2015, the forthcoming CSDDD-aligned disclosure requirements, and any applicable FCA or Companies Act ESG-disclosure frameworks. The assessment must specifically address whether JLR's existing Modern Slavery Act statement adequately covers fire-life-safety and worker-fatality risk in its South Korean and Asian supply chain, and must identify any disclosure gaps requiring remediation before the next statutory reporting deadline. The assessment must be reviewed by the Board's Audit and Risk Committee within 45 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Anchoring the legal assessment to the confirmed Korea TNC Watch Network query date and the specific SAPA criminal-charge facts prevents the assessment from being scoped as a generic ESG review, requiring counsel to address the concrete regulatory trigger created by the Anjun event.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must brief its UK HSE relationship team
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Government and Regulatory Affairs must, within 45 days of 20 March 2026, prepare and deliver a formal briefing to JLR's UK HSE relationship contacts presenting the Anjun Industrial Daejeon fire — specifically the confirmed deaths of 14 workers, the criminal prosecution of five individuals including the CEO under the Korean Serious Accidents Punishment Act, and the reported sentencing precedent established by the Aricell battery-plant CEO to 15 years imprisonment following the 2024 Hwaseong fire — as a precedent-setting event for OEM supply-chain safety-oversight obligations. The briefing must explicitly address the question of whether HSE's existing expectations for UK-headquartered OEMs' oversight of non-UK Tier-1 supplier safety performance are likely to be reinforced in light of the Daejeon prosecutions, and must seek HSE's current guidance on best practice for supply-chain fire-safety oversight. A summary of HSE's response and any recommended JLR actions must be reported to JLR's General Counsel and CPO within 60 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Referencing both the Anjun SAPA prosecutions and the Aricell sentencing precedent in the HSE briefing makes the regulatory-trajectory argument concrete, enabling a substantive dialogue about HSE expectations rather than a generic supply-chain safety update.

incidents.headline: Mazda Thailand — the warehouse system that sat unpatched for three months
incidents.summary: Mazda publicly disclosed on 19 March 2026 a December 2025 breach of its Thailand parts warehouse management system. Attackers exploited unpatched vulnerabilities to access 692 records covering employees, group company staff and business partners. ~3-month detection-to-disclosure gap aligns with Japan's APPI procedural requirements. Reported to Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission. Mazda confirmed no malware infection and no operational disruption. Remediation: patching, IP restriction, access policy revision, expanded monitoring across similar systems. Six months after JLR's September 2025 incident, the Mazda Thailand vector — unpatched warehouse system, 3-month disclosure gap, ~700 records of business-partner data — describes the typology JLR is most exposed to in its own non-core perimeter: the long tail of operational subsystems sitting inside Tier-2/3 supplier estates where JLR has data flowing but no direct control authority. UK NIS2 / UK CSIR timing would not have permitted Mazda's 3-month gap. If a Tier-2 partner gets hit on a similar vector tomorrow, the question facing the Board is not whether it gets disclosed — it's whether JLR finds out from the partner, the regulator, or the press.

graded_claims.claim: Mazda publicly disclosed a breach of its Thailand parts warehouse management system on 19 March 2026
The breach was discovered in mid-December 2025, creating an approximately three-month detection-to-disclosure gap
Attackers exploited unpatched vulnerabilities in the warehouse management system to gain unauthorised access
692 records were exposed, covering employees, group company staff and business partners including user IDs, names, email addresses, company names and business partner IDs
An external security firm assisted Mazda's internal investigation
Mazda confirmed no malware infection and no operational disruption
The incident was reported to Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission
Remediation included patching, IP restriction, access policy revision and expanded monitoring across similar systems
JLR experienced a separate breach approximately six months prior (September 2025) involving a Salesforce-linked attack by ShinyHunters
The three-month disclosure gap would not be permissible under UK NIS2 / UK CSIR timelines

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that all operational subsystems within JLR's direct and Tier-2/3 supplier estates — including warehouse management systems handling business-partner PII — are subject to a documented, enforced vulnerability management lifecycle with maximum 30-day patch currency for internet-reachable components.

Maintain continuous security monitoring coverage across non-core perimeter systems, including third-party-hosted warehouse management platforms, to reduce dwell time below the approximately three-month gap observed in the Mazda Thailand December 2025 breach.

Establish and enforce network access controls — including IP restriction and least-privilege authentication — on all operational subsystems in JLR's supply chain that store or process personal data, consistent with Mazda's disclosed post-breach remediation actions.

Ensure personal data processed by JLR's Tier-2/3 suppliers in operational subsystems — including the class of business-partner records (user IDs, names, email addresses, business partner IDs) exposed in the Mazda Thailand incident — is classified and subject to proportionate access controls.

Ensure JLR can discharge its UK GDPR Article 33 72-hour notification obligation to the ICO even when a breach originates in a Tier-2 or Tier-3 supplier's warehouse system where JLR has no direct control authority.

Establish contractual and audit mechanisms granting JLR visibility into the patch currency and security posture of Tier-2/3 supplier operational subsystems that handle JLR-linked business-partner PII, closing the control gap exposed by the Mazda Thailand WMS vector.

Ensure JLR's breach notification procedures for ICO and the NCA under UK NIS2/CSIR are triggered by supplier-originated incidents within timescales that would not have permitted the approximately 90-day Mazda Thailand detection-to-disclosure gap.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a warehouse management or parts-logistics system that stores business-partner PII and sits outside your core IT patch-management scope, you should within the next 72 hours verify the patch currency of that system against your vulnerability management SLA, confirm IP-restriction controls are active, and establish a contractual breach-notification trigger to your data protection officer — because the Mazda Thailand December 2025 breach demonstrates that a 90-day unpatched window on exactly this class of system is sufficient for a successful exploitation and a three-month regulatory disclosure gap.

velocity_signal.brief: Unpatched OT/WMS nodes in automotive Tier-2/3 estates increasingly targeted

clean_claims.claim: Mazda publicly disclosed a breach of its Thailand parts warehouse management system on 19 March 2026
The breach was discovered in mid-December 2025, creating an approximately three-month detection-to-disclosure gap
Attackers exploited unpatched vulnerabilities in the warehouse management system to gain unauthorised access
692 records were exposed, covering employees, group company staff and business partners including user IDs, names, email addresses, company names and business partner IDs
An external security firm assisted Mazda's internal investigation
JLR experienced a separate breach approximately six months prior (September 2025) involving a Salesforce-linked attack by ShinyHunters

held_claims.claim: Mazda confirmed no malware infection and no operational disruption
The incident was reported to Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission
Remediation included patching, IP restriction, access policy revision and expanded monitoring across similar systems
The three-month disclosure gap would not be permissible under UK NIS2 / UK CSIR timelines

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must implement a maximum 30-day patch SLA
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Information Security Officer must, within 60 days, amend all Tier-2 and Tier-3 supplier contracts that govern warehouse management systems (WMS) or parts-logistics platforms processing JLR-linked business-partner PII to include an explicit, auditable 30-day maximum patch SLA — measured from vendor patch release to verified deployment — with contractual right-to-audit and escalation to JLR's Supplier Risk function if any critical or high-severity vulnerability remains unpatched beyond that window. Compliance evidence required: signed contract addenda, a quarterly patch-currency attestation from each in-scope supplier, and a register of exceptions with documented risk-acceptance sign-off. This directly addresses the confirmed three-month unpatched window in Mazda Thailand's warehouse management system that enabled the December 2025 breach exposing 692 business-partner records.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control sets a patch SLA; this adaptation names the precise failure mode (three-month unpatched WMS in a Tier-2/3 Southeast Asia supplier estate), the data category at risk (business-partner PII across 692 records), and the contractual enforcement mechanism JLR must use given it has no direct operational authority over those systems.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must extend its vulnerability scanning scope to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Cyber Risk team must, within 90 days, extend its vulnerability scanning programme to enumerate and continuously assess all WMS and parts-logistics platforms operated by Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers in Southeast Asia that receive or generate JLR-linked data, using authenticated agent-based or API-integrated scanning where direct network access exists, and supplier-submitted signed vulnerability disclosure reports on a monthly cadence where it does not. The programme must produce a gap register identifying any WMS node where no patch-currency data has been received within 30 days, triggering an immediate escalation to the relevant Tier-2 contract manager. Evidence of completion: a scoped asset inventory covering Southeast Asia WMS nodes, first scan results or first supplier attestations received, and a documented escalation workflow.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control extends scanning scope; this adaptation specifies the geographic perimeter (Southeast Asia), the system class (WMS and parts-logistics, mirroring the Mazda Thailand platform type), and the dual-track method needed because JLR lacks direct network authority over Tier-2/3 environments — the exact visibility gap that left the Mazda Thailand system undetected for three months.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must configure IP restriction and access policy
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Security Governance team must, within 60 days, revise the JLR Supplier Security Annex to designate IP allowlisting and role-based access policy controls as non-negotiable baseline requirements for any operational subsystem storing one or more of the following data fields on behalf of JLR-linked individuals: user IDs, full names, email addresses, company names, or business partner IDs — the exact combination confirmed as exposed across the 692 Mazda Thailand records. The revised annex must require suppliers to document their access control configuration, provide evidence of IP restriction scope, and submit an access policy review log at least twice per year; non-compliance must trigger a supplier remediation notice within 14 days. Evidence of completion: published revised annex version, distribution confirmation to all in-scope Tier-2/3 WMS suppliers, and a compliance tracker.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control mandates IP restriction and access policy as baseline requirements; this adaptation anchors the specific data fields that trigger the obligation (matching the Mazda Thailand exposure taxonomy) and sets the review cadence and non-compliance response timeline JLR needs to operationalise the control across a supplier estate it does not directly manage.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish a monitoring cadence for its
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Security Operations Centre must, within 45 days, implement a monitoring cadence for Tier-2 and Tier-3 supplier WMS nodes — covering at minimum all Southeast Asia parts-logistics platforms that process JLR-linked PII — sufficient to detect indicators of unauthorised access within 14 calendar days of occurrence, replacing reliance on supplier self-reporting as the primary detection mechanism. Where direct log ingestion is not feasible, JLR must contractually require suppliers to forward authentication anomaly alerts and access logs to JLR's SOC or a mutually agreed SIEM within 24 hours of generation. The 14-day detection target must be validated through the tabletop exercise required under AC-CYB-025-BCH-01. Evidence of completion: updated SOC monitoring runbook naming in-scope WMS node categories, log-forwarding clauses in supplier contracts, and a baseline detection-latency metric.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control sets a detection cadence; this adaptation replaces the approximately 90-day passive dwell implicit in the Mazda Thailand December 2025 to March 2026 timeline with a specific 14-day detection target, and addresses the structural dependency on supplier self-reporting that allowed the Mazda breach to go undetected within JLR's own risk visibility.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must conduct a tabletop exercise simulating a
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Cyber Incident Response team must, within 120 days, design and execute a tabletop exercise modelled on the Mazda Thailand December 2025 scenario: a Tier-2 warehouse management system in Southeast Asia is breached via an unpatched vulnerability, approximately 700 business-partner records (user IDs, names, email addresses, business partner IDs) are exfiltrated, and JLR learns of the event only through a simulated press enquiry arriving 90 days after the breach — testing whether JLR's procedures would have detected the event earlier and whether JLR can meet its ICO 72-hour notification obligation once informed. The exercise must involve JLR Legal, Privacy, Procurement, and SOC representatives, and produce a structured after-action report identifying specific detection and notification gaps with named remediation owners and target dates. Evidence of completion: exercise scenario pack, attendance record, and published after-action report with gap register.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a tabletop exercise; this adaptation constructs the scenario parameters directly from the Mazda Thailand confirmed facts — the 90-day detection gap, the ~700-record scale, the business-partner data category, and the press-disclosure trigger — ensuring the exercise tests the specific failure chain JLR faces in its own non-core supplier perimeter rather than a generic breach scenario.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must classify the category of business-partner operational
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Data Protection Officer must, within 45 days, issue a formal data classification determination confirming that the combination of supplier user IDs, employee names, email addresses, company names, and business partner IDs — the data fields confirmed as exposed across the 692 Mazda Thailand records — constitutes personal data under UK GDPR when held in any Tier-2 or Tier-3 WMS or parts-logistics platform processing JLR-linked records, regardless of whether JLR directly operates the platform. This determination must be recorded in JLR's data processing register, must trigger a review of all supplier data processing agreements covering WMS platforms to ensure UK GDPR-consistent controls are contractually required, and must be communicated in writing to JLR's Procurement and Supplier Risk functions. Evidence of completion: signed DPO determination document, updated processing register entries, and communication log to Procurement and Supplier Risk.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires classification of business-partner operational data; this adaptation names the precise field taxonomy confirmed in the Mazda Thailand breach and resolves the jurisdictional ambiguity that arises when the processing system sits in a Tier-2/3 supplier's infrastructure outside JLR's direct control — a gap that could otherwise allow JLR to treat these records as outside its UK GDPR obligations.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must apply data minimisation controls to business-partner
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Data Protection Officer, working with Procurement and Supplier Risk, must within 90 days conduct a data minimisation review of all Tier-2 and Tier-3 supplier WMS and parts-logistics platforms in Southeast Asia that store JLR-linked records, assessing whether each of the five field types confirmed in the Mazda Thailand exposure — user IDs, employee names, email addresses, company names, business partner IDs — is operationally necessary in its current granularity and retention period, and issuing binding instructions to suppliers to delete or pseudonymise any field that cannot be justified. The review must produce a per-supplier minimisation schedule with target completion dates, and suppliers must provide written confirmation of field-level changes within 30 days of each instruction. Evidence of completion: review methodology document, per-supplier minimisation schedule, and supplier confirmation log.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires data minimisation; this adaptation targets the specific five-field combination confirmed as exposed across 692 Mazda Thailand records and imposes a field-by-field justification test on Tier-2/3 WMS platforms where JLR bears UK GDPR responsibility but lacks direct system access — the precise structural gap that amplified the Mazda breach's impact.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must verify that its data processing agreements
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Legal and Data Protection teams must, within 60 days, audit all existing data processing agreements with Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers operating WMS or parts-logistics platforms that handle JLR-linked PII to verify the presence of explicit UK GDPR Article 28 processor obligations, and must remediate any agreement that lacks a contractual requirement for the supplier to notify JLR within 72 hours of discovering any unauthorised access to personal data — including the category of user IDs, names, email addresses, and business partner IDs confirmed as exposed in the Mazda Thailand breach. Where Article 28 clauses are absent or deficient, updated agreements must be executed within a further 30 days, with Procurement withholding payment approval for non-compliant suppliers as an escalation lever. Evidence of completion: audit findings report, list of remediated agreements with execution dates, and updated DPA template incorporating the 72-hour notification clause.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires Article 28 obligations in DPAs; this adaptation responds directly to the approximately 90-day notification gap in the Mazda Thailand timeline by specifying the 72-hour contractual clock JLR needs to protect its own ICO notification obligations, and names the data field categories that trigger the obligation — anchoring the DPA requirement in the confirmed facts of the breach rather than abstract processor obligations.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must extend its third-party risk assessment programme
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Risk function must, within 60 days, extend its third-party risk assessment programme to include all Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers operating WMS or parts-logistics systems in Southeast Asia that process JLR-linked data, with patch-management currency verified at quarterly intervals using supplier-submitted signed patch registers or automated scan outputs; any WMS node where patch age for critical or high-severity vulnerabilities exceeds 30 days must be escalated to the relevant category buyer and CISO for risk-acceptance or remediation within 14 days. The programme must produce a Southeast Asia WMS supplier risk register updated quarterly, with patch-currency status as a mandatory field. Evidence of completion: scoped supplier inventory, quarterly assessment schedule, first completed risk register cycle including patch-currency data.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires quarterly patch-currency verification across Tier-2/3 suppliers; this adaptation names the specific geography (Southeast Asia), the system class (WMS and parts-logistics), and the 30-day patch threshold — directly quantifying the control gap that allowed the Mazda Thailand system to remain unpatched for approximately three months before the December 2025 breach.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must require all Tier-2/3 suppliers handling JLR-linked
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Procurement and Legal teams must, within 60 days, insert a contractual obligation into all Tier-2 and Tier-3 supplier agreements covering WMS or parts-logistics platforms that store JLR-linked business-partner PII, requiring the supplier to notify JLR's designated Data Breach Response contact within 24 hours of detecting any unauthorised access to personal data — including user IDs, names, email addresses, company names, or business partner IDs of the type confirmed exposed in the Mazda Thailand December 2025 breach — with failure to notify triggering a pre-agreed financial penalty and the right for JLR to commission an independent forensic investigation at the supplier's cost. Existing agreements must be amended via signed addenda within the 60-day window; new agreements must incorporate the clause as standard. Evidence of completion: signed addenda log, updated standard contract template, and designated notification contact published to in-scope suppliers.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires 24-hour supplier notification with penalties; this adaptation calibrates the penalty and escalation right against the specific harm demonstrated by the Mazda Thailand case — a roughly 90-day gap between breach discovery and JLR-equivalent notification — and names the exact data field categories that trigger the obligation, making the clause enforceable rather than aspirational.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must map whether a Tier-2 warehouse system
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Data Protection Officer and Legal Counsel must, within 45 days, produce a written regulatory assessment mapping whether a Tier-2 WMS breach in Southeast Asia exposing JLR-linked business-partner personal data — of the type confirmed in the Mazda Thailand December 2025 incident (user IDs, names, email addresses, company names, business partner IDs across approximately 700 records) — triggers JLR's own Article 33 UK GDPR notification duty to the ICO, and must pre-draft a completed ICO Article 33 notification template populated with the Mazda Thailand scenario parameters so that if an equivalent event occurs JLR can submit within the 72-hour clock from the point JLR itself becomes aware, irrespective of how long the supplier took to report. The assessment and template must be reviewed and approved by JLR's DPO, stored in the incident response playbook, and tested in the tabletop exercise required under AC-CYB-025-BCH-01. Evidence of completion: signed assessment document, draft ICO notification template, and playbook update confirmation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires ICO notification mapping; this adaptation addresses the specific structural problem the Mazda Thailand precedent creates for JLR — the 72-hour clock may start running from JLR's awareness date regardless of the supplier's own delay — and pre-drafts the template against the confirmed Mazda Thailand data field taxonomy so JLR is not drafting from scratch under time pressure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must document the regulatory notification sequence —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Legal and Compliance teams must, within 60 days, produce a documented multi-jurisdiction notification sequence covering the scenario in which a Tier-2 WMS supplier in Thailand (or comparable Southeast Asia jurisdiction) suffers a breach exposing JLR-linked personal data, specifying: (a) JLR's ICO notification obligation and 72-hour trigger point under UK GDPR Article 33; (b) any NCA notification obligation under UK NIS2 / CSIR if JLR's own network or services are in scope; and (c) how the local controller's notification timeline — which in the Mazda Thailand case extended to approximately three months under Japan's APPI procedural framework — interacts with and does not substitute for JLR's UK obligations. The document must name the internal role responsible for initiating each notification stream, define the evidence threshold for triggering each, and be incorporated into JLR's Incident Response Playbook with a version-control date. Evidence of completion: signed notification sequence document, playbook integration confirmation, and Legal sign-off record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control documents the regulatory notification sequence; this adaptation resolves the multi-jurisdictional collision specific to the Mazda Thailand typology — where a local APPI-equivalent timeline of approximately three months created a disclosure gap that would have been unlawful under UK GDPR and NIS2 — ensuring JLR's UK obligations are tracked independently of whatever the supplier's local regulator permits.

incidents.headline: Foxconn × Nitrogen — 8 TB on a leak site, the same vector that hit JLR in September
incidents.summary: Nitrogen ransomware group listed Foxconn (Hon Hai) on its NitroBlog leak site on 11 May 2026, claiming exfiltration of ~8 TB of data across >11 million files. Foxconn publicly confirmed on 12 May. Initial-access vector: malvertising via SEO-poisoned ads pushing trojanised IT-admin tools (WinSCP, PuTTY clones). Lateral movement through domain controller to ESXi management plane. ESXi encryption deployed; a bug in Nitrogen's encryptor means recovery requires backup. NA operations disrupted at Mount Pleasant WI and Houston TX. Foxconn confirmed customer data including Apple, Intel, Nvidia, AMD references in leaked samples. Halcyon disclosed a parallel supply-chain compromise of its Trivy security tool the same week. The Nitrogen kill chain — malvertising → trojanised IT admin tool → domain controller → ESXi management plane → bulk encryption — is not novel. It is the same kill chain pattern that materialised at JLR in September 2025. Six months on, the question facing JLR's CISO is not whether new variants exist (they do) but whether the IT operations admin perimeter — the specific surface that was successfully exploited last time — has materially hardened in ways that would deny Nitrogen's exact playbook on a re-run.

graded_claims.claim: Nitrogen ransomware group listed Foxconn on its NitroBlog leak site on 11 May 2026
Foxconn publicly confirmed the cyberattack on 12 May 2026 via spokesperson statement to press
Nitrogen claims exfiltration of approximately 8 TB of data spanning more than 11 million files
NA operations disrupted at Mount Pleasant WI and Houston TX
Initial-access vector was malvertising via SEO-poisoned ads pushing trojanised WinSCP and PuTTY installers
Lateral movement proceeded through domain controller to ESXi management plane, then bulk encryption deployed
A coding mistake in Nitrogen's ESXi encryptor corrupts the public key, making decryption impossible even after payment
Leaked samples reference Apple, Intel, Nvidia, AMD, Google, and Dell customer project data
Foxconn declined to confirm whether customer data was stolen or whether a ransom demand was issued
Halcyon itself was within scope of a parallel Trivy (CVE-2026-33634) supply chain compromise in March 2026, though no customer data was found affected
Unreleased Apple product designs were not observed in public sample set, consistent with Foxconn's Mount Pleasant plant primarily producing televisions and data servers
The same Nitrogen kill chain — malvertising to trojanised IT admin tool to domain controller to ESXi — was used against JLR in September 2025

internal.name: JLR North America Operations (IT/OT Infrastructure)

internal.impact_rationale: Confirmed prior victim of identical Nitrogen kill chain; re-entry risk is materially elevated until admin surface hardens.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Tabletop an exact Nitrogen kill-chain re-run against JLR NA ESXi clusters within 72 hours, validating WinSCP and PuTTY install-path controls.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure identity and access controls prevent malvertising-delivered credential compromise from achieving domain-controller-level privilege escalation, as demonstrated by Nitrogen's WinSCP/PuTTY kill chain at Foxconn and previously at JLR in September 2025.

Detect and contain ransomware lateral movement across JLR's domain-controller-to-ESXi management-plane path before bulk encryption is deployed, based on the confirmed Nitrogen attack sequence of May 2026.

Ensure backup-driven recovery capability for ESXi environments is tested and validated, given Coveware's documented finding that Nitrogen's ESXi encryptor coding error renders paid decryption mathematically impossible.

Harden endpoint controls at JLR IT-admin workstations against SEO-poisoned malvertising delivering trojanised WinSCP, PuTTY, and AnyDesk installers, the confirmed Nitrogen initial-access vector at Foxconn.

Identify and classify data held on JLR systems that, if exfiltrated, would constitute a notifiable breach under UK GDPR Article 33, in light of the 8 TB exfiltration template established by the Foxconn incident.

Ensure third-party supplier data (e.g. OEM partner schematics, customer project files) stored on JLR infrastructure is scoped and contractually protected so that exfiltration exposure is bounded and reportable.

Assess whether IT managed-service providers with privileged access to JLR Active Directory — specifically TCS and Wipro — have controls sufficient to deny Nitrogen's domain-controller lateral-movement path.

adaptive_master_controls.adaptive_requirement: Block all search-advertisement-served downloads of WinSCP, PuTTY, AnyDesk, and Advanced IP Scanner at JLR's web proxy, enforcing installs from JLR's approved internal software repository only, as Nitrogen's Foxconn initial access was confirmed via malvertising of these exact tools.

adaptive_master_controls.adaptive_requirement: Implement network segmentation that prevents JLR domain controllers from directly reaching ESXi management interfaces, closing the specific DC-to-hypervisor pivot path Nitrogen used at Foxconn Mount Pleasant in May 2026.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Block all search-advertisement-served downloads of WinSCP
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 72 hours of this control's issue date, JLR's IT Security Operations team must verify that JLR's web proxy (covering all NA IT-admin endpoints at Castle Bromwich, Solihull, and any NA satellite sites) actively blocks download of WinSCP, PuTTY, AnyDesk, and Advanced IP Scanner from all sources other than JLR's internal software distribution repository, using URL-category rules targeting search-advertisement traffic domains and file-hash blocking for known trojanised installer hashes published in Halcyon's May 2026 Nitrogen-Foxconn IOC set. Proxy rule deployment must be evidenced by a signed configuration extract and a proxy-log sample confirming at least one ad-network-sourced download attempt was blocked within a 24-hour test window; the JLR CISO must countersign the evidence pack as closed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control mandates blocking ad-served downloads of these tools broadly; this adaptation anchors the specific four tools to the confirmed Foxconn initial-access vector, requires targeting of search-advertisement traffic specifically (the confirmed delivery mechanism), and ties the IOC set to the named May 2026 Halcyon disclosure rather than a generic threat-intelligence feed.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement network segmentation that prevents JLR domain controllers
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 7 calendar days, JLR's Network Security and Infrastructure teams must produce and review a firewall-rule matrix confirming that no JLR domain controller — in either the UK production estate or the NA operational environment — holds a permitted routed path to any ESXi management interface (VMware vCenter, ESXi DCUI, or iLO/iDRAC management ports 443, 902, 5900, 623) without traversal of a dedicated privileged-access workstation (PAW) segment enforced by an explicit deny-all default policy; any gap identified must be remediated within a further 7 days, with evidence of remediation submitted to the JLR CISO as a firewall-change ticket referencing this control. This requirement is driven by the reported DC-to-ESXi management-plane pivot in the Nitrogen-Foxconn incident of May 2026; if that lateral-movement path is subsequently confirmed as having also been used in the September 2025 JLR incident, the same evidence pack will serve as documentation of closure for both events.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for DC-to-hypervisor segmentation in the abstract; this adaptation specifies the exact management ports, the PAW-segment enforcement mechanism, the dual-jurisdiction scope (UK and NA), the 14-day total remediation window, and embeds a conditional clause covering the held claim that the same path was used at JLR in September 2025.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy DLL-sideloading detection rules tuned to WinSCP and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 5 business days, JLR's Threat Detection Engineering team must deploy SIEM and EDR detection rules — derived directly from the DLL-sideloading IOCs published by Halcyon in their May 2026 Nitrogen-Foxconn campaign disclosure — to all IT-admin workstations in JLR's NA environment, with rules specifically tuned to detect unsigned or mismatched DLLs loaded by WinSCP.exe and PuTTY.exe process trees; rule deployment must be validated by a purple-team replay of at least one documented IOC against an NA IT-admin test endpoint, with a detection-confirmed log extract and a signed sign-off from the JLR SOC Lead submitted as evidence of completion within 10 business days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for DLL-sideloading detection against WinSCP and PuTTY clones broadly; this adaptation requires the rules to be sourced explicitly from the named Halcyon May 2026 IOC disclosure for the Foxconn campaign, restricts initial deployment scope to the highest-risk NA IT-admin workstation population, and mandates purple-team validation rather than passive rule push.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Validate that JLR's ESXi backup snapshots are immutable
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 5 business days, JLR's Infrastructure and Business Continuity teams must execute a live recovery test of at least two ESXi VMs — one from the NA operational environment and one from the UK production estate — from offline or air-gapped backup snapshots, completing the restoration without use of any encryption key held on the production vCenter or domain controller, and documenting the achieved RTO and RPO against the current DR SLA; the test must be witnessed by the JLR CISO or delegate, and the evidence pack — comprising a test plan, restoration log, and RTO/RPO attestation — must be retained as the closure artefact for this control, with any gap against the DR SLA triggering an immediate remediation plan within 3 further business days. This control is anchored to the reported finding that Nitrogen's ESXi encryptor for the Foxconn attack irrecoverably corrupts ciphertext, rendering decryption impossible even after payment, making verified backup recoverability the sole reliable recovery path.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for immutable and segmented ESXi backups in the abstract; this adaptation requires a live tested restoration exercise rather than a paper attestation, ties the urgency directly to the reported irrecoverability finding from the Foxconn encryptor bug, and specifies the dual-jurisdiction test scope with explicit RTO/RPO measurement against the live DR SLA.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Run a tabletop exercise within 14 days simulating
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 14 calendar days of this control's issue date (no later than 11 June 2026), JLR's CISO must convene a facilitated tabletop exercise with mandatory attendance from IT Security Operations, Network Infrastructure, SOC, the NA site leads, and at least one representative from each third-party managed-service provider with privileged access to JLR's AD or ESXi environments; the scenario must replay the confirmed Nitrogen kill chain — a JLR NA IT-admin engineer downloads a trojanised WinSCP or PuTTY installer via a search-advertisement result, a RAT establishes persistence, lateral movement reaches a JLR domain controller, and ESXi bulk encryption is initiated — and the exercise must formally evaluate whether each post-September-2025 hardening measure (proxy block, PAW enforcement, EDR rule, backup isolation) would have interrupted the chain at its documented step, with a written findings report submitted to the JLR CISO within 5 business days of the exercise date identifying any residual gaps.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a tabletop exercise simulating the Nitrogen kill chain; this adaptation fixes the mandatory completion date to 14 days from issue (making it time-bound against the Foxconn incident date), prescribes mandatory attendees including third-party MSP representatives, requires the exercise to explicitly evaluate each post-September-2025 control against the specific kill-chain steps, and mandates a structured findings report rather than informal discussion notes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Audit all JLR Active Directory privileged accounts accessible
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 5 business days, JLR's Identity and Access Management team must produce a full inventory of all Active Directory privileged accounts — domain admin, schema admin, enterprise admin, and ESXi vCenter admin — that are currently accessible by TCS and Wipro managed-service engineers across JLR's NA and UK environments, and must confirm in writing to the JLR CISO that no such account holds standing (always-on) domain-admin or vCenter-admin privileges; any account found with standing elevation must be converted to a just-in-time (JIT) model with a maximum session duration of 4 hours and a mandatory approval workflow within a further 5 business days, with a completed IAM configuration extract and access-log sample provided as closure evidence. This requirement is anchored to the reported Nitrogen lateral-movement path at Foxconn in May 2026, in which standing privileged-account access enabled progression from initial compromise to domain controller and ESXi management plane; the same kill chain is reported to have been used against JLR in September 2025, and any residual standing MSP-held privilege represents a directly replicable entry path.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for an audit of MSP privileged accounts and JIT enforcement broadly; this adaptation names TCS and Wipro specifically as the in-scope MSP population, restricts the session ceiling to 4 hours, scopes the inventory to both AD domain-tier and ESXi vCenter accounts (the two tiers traversed in the reported kill chain), and includes the conditional reference to the September 2025 JLR incident as a contingency that elevates urgency if confirmed.

incidents.headline: April 2026 — 801 ransomware claims, the highest month on record
incidents.summary: CYFIRMA logs 801 ransomware incidents in April 2026 — sector record. Manufacturing alone 208 in March 2026. Halcyon discloses its own Trivy security-tool supply-chain compromise. Automotive contributes 15 incidents in April. Pwn2Own Automotive 2026 closes with 76 zero-days disclosed across SDV, IVI and EV-charging stacks — all in JLR's product roadmap. Sector base-rate ~26 ransomware events per day. Three Lloyd's syndicates re-quote JLR's cyber policy at 2.4× the prior premium with halved limit. Cyber-insurance market hardens materially. JLR's FY24/25 register describes cyber as "rising". The April 2026 data says cyber is now a structural sector base-rate: ~26 ransomware events per day, manufacturing the most-targeted sector, JLR a uniquely re-rated insurance buyer post-September 2025. The financial exposure is no longer the single-event cost — it is the structural shift from external risk transfer (insurance) to internal retention (capital). Three Lloyd's syndicates re-quoting JLR at 2.4× premium with halved limit is the empirical signal that the cover assumptions in JLR's controls posture need refresh against a market that has moved.

The UK government supported a £1.5 billion loan guarantee to help restore JLR's supply chain following the September 2025 cyberattack.
JLR was losing approximately 1,000 vehicles per day at its British operations, with losses reportedly ~£50 million per week, during the September 2025 attack.

incidents.headline: Omnibus I in force — JLR stays in scope while 80% of peers fall out
incidents.summary: Directive (EU) 2026/470 enters into force. CSRD scope narrowed to >1,000 employees / €450M turnover; CSDDD to >5,000 / €1.5B. CSDDD compliance pushed to 26 July 2029; max CSDDD fine reduced from 5% to 3% of global net turnover. Member State transposition deadline 26 July 2028. JLR clears both thresholds comfortably. Approximately 80% of previously in-scope companies fall out of CSRD; CSDDD scope narrows even further. The mandate to adopt a climate transition plan has been removed from CSDDD, though CSRD transition-plan disclosure persists. Sector-specific reporting standards are now voluntary. The character of regulatory risk has inverted in March 2026. The fear in the FY24/25 register was "too many rules, changing too fast." The new reality is "fewer rules for our competitors, same rules for us." JLR's existing ESG disclosure infrastructure — KPMG-assured, SBTi-aligned, supplier due-diligence under Kass — is now a structural cost-base that ~50,000 newly-exempt EU companies, including significant luxury peers, no longer carry. The strategic question for the Board is whether to hold the position as a brand differentiator (luxury buyer ESG-conscious) or seek UK transposition relief (preserve margin parity).

Maximum CSDDD fine reduced from 5% to 3% of global net turnover.
The obligation to adopt and put into effect a climate transition plan under CSDDD has been removed.
Approximately 80% of previously in-scope CSRD companies fall out of scope under the Omnibus thresholds.

JLR's ESG disclosure infrastructure is KPMG-assured and SBTi-aligned, with supplier due-diligence under a platform named 'Kass'.
Sector-specific CSRD reporting standards are now voluntary rather than mandatory.
