adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain a live regulatory-horizon register that tracks UK
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Regulatory Affairs must, within 30 days of this control's issue, establish and thereafter maintain a live regulatory-horizon register covering UK transposition of Directive (EU) 2026/470 ('Omnibus I'), distinguishing JLR's UK-domiciled CSRD obligations (monitored against any UK equivalent transposition deadline) from the EU-subsidiary CSDDD exposure governed by the confirmed 26 July 2028 Member State transposition deadline and 26 July 2029 application date. The register must be reviewed quarterly by the General Counsel, flag any UK BEIS/DBT consultation documents within five business days of publication, and be tabled at each Audit and Risk Committee meeting as a standing agenda item until at least 31 December 2028. Evidence of completion is a version-controlled register entry in JLR's GRC system showing the current transposition status of each jurisdiction, last-reviewed date, and accountable owner.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for horizon-scanning; this adaptation anchors it to the specific dual-track risk created by Omnibus I — JLR faces divergent timelines and obligations depending on whether the exposure is UK-domiciled or routed through an EU subsidiary, a distinction that does not exist for purely domestic incidents.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a formal scope-confirmation assessment confirming JLR clears
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Financial Officer must commission and sign off a formal scope-confirmation memorandum, within 45 days of Directive (EU) 2026/470 entering into force on 18 March 2026, confirming JLR's employee headcount and net annual turnover against the revised CSRD thresholds (>1,000 employees / >€450M net turnover) and CSDDD thresholds (>5,000 employees / >€1.5B net worldwide turnover) using audited FY2024/25 financial data. The memorandum must be reviewed by KPMG as part of their sustainability assurance engagement and presented to the Board at its next scheduled meeting, with a clear conclusion — stated as confirmed fact or as a contingency pending auditor sign-off — on JLR's in-scope status under each directive. Evidence of completion is the countersigned memorandum lodged in the Board papers archive and a written acknowledgement from KPMG that the scope determination has been noted in the assurance file.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a scope assessment; this adaptation is anchored to the specific Omnibus I thresholds, requires resolution of the currently soft claim about JLR's qualifying status against audited figures, and names KPMG as the necessary third-party validator given their existing assurance relationship.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Engage JLR's external legal counsel and the UK
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The General Counsel must, by 30 September 2026, commission JLR's external legal counsel to produce a written opinion assessing (a) whether UK BEIS/DBT intends to transpose Omnibus-equivalent scope relief into any UK sustainability reporting regime, and (b) the legal pathway and timeline for JLR to benefit from any such relief ahead of the EU Member State transposition deadline of 26 July 2028. The opinion must include a quantified margin-recovery scenario — prepared jointly with the CFO's office using FY2024/25 ESG infrastructure cost data — modelling the annual cost saving if JLR were exempted from CSRD-equivalent UK obligations. The completed opinion and financial model must be presented to the Board Strategy Committee by 31 October 2026 to inform the hold-vs-relief decision. Evidence of completion is the dated counsel opinion, the cost model, and the Board Strategy Committee minute recording its receipt.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for regulatory engagement; this adaptation is specific to the Omnibus I-created asymmetry where JLR remains in EU scope while UK transposition remains uncertain, requiring a concrete legal and financial deliverable that directly informs the Board's strategic decision before the EU transposition clock runs.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update JLR's Board risk register to reflect the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Risk Officer must update JLR's Board risk register, at the next scheduled Audit and Risk Committee meeting following 18 March 2026 and no later than 30 June 2026, to close or materially reclassify the FY2024/25 risk entry characterised as 'excessive and rapidly changing regulatory burden' and replace it with a new risk entry titled 'Omnibus I cost asymmetry: ESG compliance cost persists for JLR while approximately 80% of previously in-scope EU peers, and an unquantified proportion of luxury automotive competitors, have exited mandatory CSRD scope.' The new entry must include a gross and net risk rating, name the CFO as risk owner, quantify the annual cost of JLR's retained ESG infrastructure as the financial exposure metric, and cross-reference the hold-vs-relief strategic decision (MC-STR-021). Evidence of completion is the updated risk register entry, version-controlled with change history, tabled in the ARC minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a register update; this adaptation captures the specific directional inversion — from 'too many rules' to 'competitors exempt, JLR in-scope cost asymmetry' — that is the defining risk character of Omnibus I for JLR, and links it explicitly to the confirmed fact that approximately 80% of CSRD peers have now exited scope.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review whether JLR's KPMG sustainability assurance contract scope
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The CFO and Head of Sustainability Reporting must, by 31 July 2026, convene a joint review with KPMG to assess whether JLR's existing sustainability assurance contract — currently calibrated to reasonable-assurance standards — should be re-scoped to limited assurance in line with the Omnibus I shift, and must produce a written cost-benefit analysis quantifying the fee saving against any risk to investor-relations credibility or CSRD compliance. The outcome of the review — either a contract re-negotiation instruction to KPMG or a documented decision to retain reasonable assurance with stated rationale — must be approved by the CFO and reported to the Audit and Risk Committee by 30 September 2026. Evidence of completion is the cost-benefit analysis, the ARC paper recording the decision, and (if re-negotiation is approved) a signed contract amendment with KPMG.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses assurance re-scoping; this adaptation anchors the review to the specific Omnibus I policy change from reasonable to limited assurance, names KPMG as the incumbent assurer, and requires a quantified cost-saving analysis rather than a generic review, making the commercial rationale explicit and auditable.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Confirm that JLR's CSRD transition-plan disclosure (which persists
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Sustainability Reporting must, before the FY2025 sustainability report is submitted for KPMG assurance, insert a clearly labelled explanatory note in the report's climate governance section that explicitly distinguishes (a) JLR's CSRD-mandated climate transition plan disclosure under ESRS E1 — which continues to apply unchanged — from (b) the CSDDD obligation to adopt and implement a climate transition plan, which was removed by Directive (EU) 2026/470 effective 18 March 2026 and therefore does not apply to JLR's FY2025 obligations. This note must be reviewed by external legal counsel and approved by the General Counsel before assurance submission; any draft that conflates the two obligations or implies JLR has discharged a CSDDD transition-plan obligation must be returned for correction. Evidence of completion is the approved final text of the explanatory note, counsel's written sign-off, and the KPMG assurance letter referencing the distinction.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses transition-plan obligations; this adaptation is specific to the Omnibus I removal of the CSDDD implementation obligation and the concrete risk that JLR's FY2025 sustainability report — drafted partly under the prior regulatory framework — conflates the surviving CSRD disclosure requirement with the now-deleted CSDDD action obligation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether sector-specific ESRS standards — now voluntary
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Sustainability Reporting, supported by the Investor Relations Director, must by 31 August 2026 produce a written assessment of each sector-specific ESRS standard currently embedded in JLR's KPMG-assured sustainability reporting, categorising each as (a) retained as voluntary disclosure with stated investor-relations or brand rationale, (b) retained pending a formal Board decision on the hold-vs-relief strategic question, or (c) proposed for removal to reduce reporting cost, with an estimated annual saving attached to each removal proposal. The assessment must be presented to the CFO and General Counsel for approval and then to the Board Strategy Committee alongside the hold-vs-relief decision framework (see AC-STR-021-ESG-01). Evidence of completion is the categorised assessment document, the two sign-offs, and the Board Strategy Committee minute recording a decision on each category.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses sector-specific standard retention; this adaptation is anchored to the Omnibus I reclassification of these standards as voluntary, requires a standard-by-standard rather than all-or-nothing decision, and links the outcome explicitly to JLR's broader strategic positioning question rather than treating it as a standalone reporting exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Evaluate whether JLR's SBTi-aligned science-based targets should be
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Investor Relations Director and Head of Sustainability Reporting must, in the FY2025 Annual Report narrative (investor and stakeholder sections), include an explicit passage positioning JLR's science-based targets — currently aligned to SBTi — as a voluntary strategic commitment that JLR maintains in full despite Directive (EU) 2026/470 removing the equivalent mandatory obligation from approximately 80% of previously in-scope EU peers; the passage must be factually grounded in the confirmed Omnibus I scope changes and must not make unverified comparative claims about named competitors. The draft passage must be reviewed by the General Counsel for legal accuracy and by the Board ESG Sub-Committee for strategic alignment before the Annual Report is filed. Evidence of completion is the approved passage in the filed Annual Report and the sign-off record from both reviewers.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for voluntary differentiation messaging; this adaptation is specific to JLR's SBTi-aligned targets, grounds the messaging claim in the confirmed Omnibus I fact that approximately 80% of peers have exited mandatory scope, and imposes the factual guardrail against unverified competitor comparisons that the generic control does not address.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Present the Board with a structured hold-vs-relief decision
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The CFO and Chief Strategy Officer must jointly deliver to the Board, at its next scheduled meeting after 30 September 2026 and no later than 31 October 2026, a structured hold-vs-relief decision paper that: (a) states the total annual cost of JLR's retained ESG infrastructure — covering KPMG sustainability assurance fees, internal sustainability reporting headcount, and, if the Kass supplier due-diligence platform claim is confirmed, its licence and operational cost — against a baseline of what that infrastructure would cost if JLR were exempt under Omnibus I thresholds; (b) presents an estimated brand-premium valuation for JLR's ESG compliance position among its luxury buyer cohort, drawing on available market research; and (c) sets out at least two discrete strategic options (hold full position; seek UK transposition relief) with a recommendation. The Board must record a formal decision on the strategic question in its minutes. Evidence of completion is the decision paper lodged in the Board papers archive and the minute recording the Board's decision.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a cost-vs-brand-value framework; this adaptation specifies the exact cost components to be quantified (KPMG assurance, headcount, Kass platform subject to confirmation), the luxury-cohort framing of the brand-premium side, and the Board-decision timeline tied to the Omnibus I regulatory window, preventing the exercise from remaining an indefinite analytical project.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Scenario-plan the competitive implications if one or more
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Strategy Officer must, by 31 August 2026, commission and present to the Board Strategy Committee a competitive scenario analysis examining the implications for JLR's market position if one or more luxury automotive peers — including but not limited to those with fewer than 5,000 employees or below €1.5B worldwide turnover under the CSDDD thresholds set by Directive (EU) 2026/470 — use the Omnibus I exemptions to exit mandatory CSRD reporting, thereby reducing their ESG compliance cost base relative to JLR's retained obligation. The analysis must model at least two scenarios (one peer exits, multiple peers exit) and must quantify the implied annual per-vehicle cost disadvantage for JLR under each scenario using FY2024/25 volume and ESG cost data. Evidence of completion is the dated scenario document, the Board Strategy Committee presentation record, and the CFO's sign-off on the cost assumptions used.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for competitive scenario-planning; this adaptation is anchored to the specific Omnibus I CSDDD threshold numbers that determine which luxury peers may exit scope, requires a per-vehicle cost-disadvantage metric that is actionable for pricing and margin strategy, and avoids making confirmed claims about specific named competitors by framing the analysis around threshold-based eligibility.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Reconfigure JLR's supplier due-diligence programme ('Kass') to reflect
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Procurement Officer must, by 30 June 2026, direct a re-tiering review of JLR's supplier due-diligence engagement model to ensure that data and disclosure requests issued to suppliers with fewer than 1,000 employees do not exceed the simplified VSME standard established under Directive (EU) 2026/470, reflecting the Omnibus I value-chain cap. The review must produce a revised supplier segmentation matrix — distinguishing suppliers above and below the 1,000-employee threshold — and updated data-request templates calibrated to each tier; if the Kass platform is confirmed as JLR's due-diligence system, the Kass configuration must be updated to enforce the tiered request limits before any new supplier engagement cycle commences. The revised matrix, templates, and (if applicable) Kass configuration change must be approved by the General Counsel and evidence of completion lodged in the GRC system by 30 June 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses value-chain data request limits; this adaptation is specific to the Omnibus I 1,000-employee threshold triggering the VSME cap, requires a concrete supplier segmentation matrix and template update rather than a policy review, and includes a conditional Kass platform configuration step tied to the confirmation of that system's use.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update JLR's standard supplier contract templates to remove
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The General Counsel must, by 31 May 2026, instruct JLR's procurement legal team to audit all live and template supplier contracts for clauses requiring suppliers to adopt, maintain, or report against a climate transition plan in fulfilment of CSDDD obligations, and to issue revised standard contract templates from which those clauses have been removed, reflecting the confirmed removal of the CSDDD climate transition plan obligation by Directive (EU) 2026/470 effective 18 March 2026. Human-rights and environmental due-diligence clauses must be explicitly preserved and flagged as retained in the revised templates. A contract amendment notice must be issued to all suppliers currently subject to the removed clauses, and a clause-change log must be maintained for audit purposes. Evidence of completion is the signed-off revised template set, the clause-change log, and confirmation from the procurement legal team that all in-flight contract negotiations have adopted the revised templates.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for contract template updates; this adaptation is specific to the Omnibus I removal of the CSDDD climate transition plan obligation as a confirmed legal fact, requires an audit of live contracts — not just templates — to catch already-executed agreements, and explicitly mandates preservation of the human-rights and environmental clauses to prevent over-correction.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a gap analysis of JLR's CSDDD due-diligence
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Procurement Officer, supported by the Head of Sustainability and external legal counsel, must by 31 December 2026 complete a gap analysis of JLR's existing CSDDD due-diligence programme against the revised risk-based approach required by Directive (EU) 2026/470, identifying which value-chain tiers and supplier categories represent the most material adverse human-rights and environmental impact risks and therefore warrant retained or intensified due-diligence effort ahead of the 26 July 2029 CSDDD application date. The gap analysis must produce a prioritised remediation roadmap with discrete milestones for 2027 and 2028, calibrated to the 26 July 2028 Member State transposition deadline, and must be approved by the General Counsel and presented to the Audit and Risk Committee. Evidence of completion is the dated gap-analysis report, the remediation roadmap with milestone owners, and the ARC minute recording receipt and approval.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a due-diligence gap analysis; this adaptation anchors it to the specific Omnibus I application date of 26 July 2029 and the 26 July 2028 transposition deadline as the twin planning horizons, requires a tiered materiality prioritisation rather than a uniform programme review, and produces a milestone roadmap that makes JLR's compliance trajectory auditable against the confirmed statutory calendar.

incidents.headline: Stellantis × ZF — the payment dispute that halted Toluca
incidents.summary: A payment dispute between Stellantis and Germany-based ZF Chassis Modules (Windsor) — disclosed via a March 2026 lawsuit filed in Michigan — has halted production at Stellantis' Toluca plant in Mexico for more than two weeks and was threatening Canadian operations as of 27 March 2026. The dispute concerns supplier payments; ZF's lawsuit details substantial unpaid invoices. Stellantis Toluca paused. Canadian operations at risk. ZF Friedrichshafen separately announced 14,000 job cuts by 2030; Bosch is cutting 22,000. The case crystallises a longer-term pattern: OEM cash management is now pressuring Tier-1 supplier financial viability in ways that produce non-cyber, non-fire production halts. Kass / Secure26 surfaces Tier-1 financial distress. It does not surface the inverse — a financially stable Tier-1 (ZF Friedrichshafen is solvent) taking contract action over an OEM's working-capital position. Stellantis × ZF establishes that Tier-1s now have both the legal posture and the operational willingness to halt OEM production lines over payment terms, not just over insolvency. JLR's mature payment terms with major Tier-1s sit inside this exposure. The control gap is not detecting Tier-1 weakness — it's detecting Tier-1 willingness to use the courts.

graded_claims.claim: Stellantis filed a lawsuit against ZF Chassis Modules in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Michigan on March 25, 2026.
Production at Stellantis' Toluca assembly plant was halted beginning March 14, 2026.
Stellantis alleged it paid more than $26 million and agreed to price increases in December 2025 to resolve a prior stop-ship situation.
ZF Chassis Modules subsequently demanded an additional $70 million lump-sum cash payment and further price increases.
ZF Chassis Modules is a joint venture between ZF Friedrichshafen and Foxconn, now operating as ZF Foxconn Chassis Modules.
The Toluca plant produces Jeep Compass and Jeep Cherokee models for North and Latin American markets and employs around 2,500 workers.
ZF's parts stoppage also threatened operations at Stellantis' Windsor, Ontario plant, which employs 5,500 workers.
A Michigan judge issued a temporary restraining order on March 25, 2026, compelling ZF to resume parts shipments.
Other suppliers stated in court filings that Stellantis supply deals are not 'requirement contracts,' meaning suppliers have no legal obligation to ship parts.
Stellantis was managing the dispute amid a broader turnaround following $26 billion in charges tied to a reset of its EV strategy in 2025.
JLR's standard supplier payment terms are 60 days post-invoice, and JLR introduced an early-payment financing scheme for suppliers during its October 2025 manufacturing restart.
JLR's cyber incident since August 2025 pushed Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers in its chain to the brink of collapse, with banks demanding personal guarantees.
ZF Friedrichshafen separately announced plans to cut 14,000 jobs by 2030.

internal.name: JLR Supplier Finance & Procurement — Solihull and Halewood Operations
internal.reason: JLR's 60-day standard payment terms, already stressed by the 2025 cyber incident, are directly analogous to the Stellantis working-capital posture ZF litigated.
internal.impact_rationale: JLR's publicly disclosed 60-day payment terms match the exact structural vulnerability ZF exploited at Stellantis.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Convene a payment-terms audit across all Tier-1 chassis and suspension module suppliers, stress-testing against a ZF-style stop-ship ultimatum within 30 days.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure JLR's Tier-1 supplier payment terms are structured and monitored to prevent a ZF-style stop-ship ultimatum from halting production at Solihull or Halewood.
Ensure JLR's supplier contracts with chassis and suspension module providers contain enforceable continuity-of-supply obligations that survive a payment pricing dispute.
Ensure JLR maintains current due diligence on the financial and legal posture of Tier-1 suppliers, particularly those operating as joint ventures with demonstrated stop-ship willingness.
Ensure JLR's supplier dependency mapping identifies single-source module suppliers whose withdrawal would halt a JLR production line within 72 hours.
Ensure JLR's operational continuity plans include a supplier-payment-dispute scenario as a named non-cyber, non-fire production-halt trigger.

Ensure JLR's working-capital and supplier payment governance is stress-tested against the scenario where a Tier-1 demands a lump-sum cash payment exceeding JLR's standard 60-day terms as a condition of continued supply.

Ensure JLR's legal and regulatory obligations arising from a UK production halt caused by a Tier-1 payment dispute — including employment law notification thresholds — are mapped and pre-cleared with counsel.

clean_claims.claim: Stellantis filed a lawsuit against ZF Chassis Modules in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Michigan on March 25, 2026.
Production at Stellantis' Toluca assembly plant was halted beginning March 14, 2026.
Stellantis alleged it paid more than $26 million and agreed to price increases in December 2025 to resolve a prior stop-ship situation.
ZF Chassis Modules subsequently demanded an additional $70 million lump-sum cash payment and further price increases.
ZF Chassis Modules is a joint venture between ZF Friedrichshafen and Foxconn, now operating as ZF Foxconn Chassis Modules.
The Toluca plant produces Jeep Compass and Jeep Cherokee models for North and Latin American markets and employs around 2,500 workers.
ZF's parts stoppage also threatened operations at Stellantis' Windsor, Ontario plant, which employs 5,500 workers.
A Michigan judge issued a temporary restraining order on March 25, 2026, compelling ZF to resume parts shipments.
Stellantis was managing the dispute amid a broader turnaround following $26 billion in charges tied to a reset of its EV strategy in 2025.
JLR's standard supplier payment terms are 60 days post-invoice, and JLR introduced an early-payment financing scheme for suppliers during its October 2025 manufacturing restart.
JLR's cyber incident since August 2025 pushed Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers in its chain to the brink of collapse, with banks demanding personal guarantees.
ZF Friedrichshafen separately announced plans to cut 14,000 jobs by 2030.

held_claims.claim: Other suppliers stated in court filings that Stellantis supply deals are not 'requirement contracts,' meaning suppliers have no legal obligation to ship parts.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must audit all Tier-1 chassis and suspension
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must, within 30 calendar days of 25 March 2026, commission a targeted legal review of all active Tier-1 chassis and suspension module supply agreements — specifically those covering the MLA platform at Solihull and equivalent platforms at Halewood and Nitra — to determine whether each agreement constitutes a 'requirements contract' enforceable under English law and, where applicable, under the law of the supplier's home jurisdiction, with written counsel opinion delivered per agreement. The review must document, for each agreement, whether JLR has a legally enforceable right to compel continued shipment in the event of a payment dispute, and must identify any agreements that permit unilateral stop-ship action of the kind ZF Chassis Modules executed against Stellantis beginning 12 March 2026. A remediation register citing each deficient agreement, the responsible category manager, and a contractual cure deadline must be presented to JLR's General Counsel and CPO no later than day 45.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for enforceability audits broadly; this adaptation anchors the obligation to the specific MLA platform supply chain, the English-law and multi-jurisdictional complexity of JLR's supplier base, and the precise stop-ship mechanism exposed by the Stellantis v. ZF Michigan proceeding, rather than treating contract enforceability as a binary compliance checkbox.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must establish a payment-dispute escalation protocol specifically
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's VP Procurement and General Counsel must jointly ratify, within 45 days of 25 March 2026, a Payment-Dispute Escalation Protocol covering all single-source module suppliers to the Solihull MLA, Halewood, and Nitra lines, which defines in writing the monetary threshold — set at a sterling equivalent of £55 million lump-sum demand or any demand exceeding 15% of the supplier's prior-12-month invoice value with JLR, whichever is lower — at which JLR's legal hold procedure and alternative-source activation sequence are automatically triggered. The protocol must designate named role-holders (not generic titles) accountable within the first 24 hours of a demand being received, specify the communications lockdown procedure to prevent unauthorised commitments to the demanding supplier, and require that the JLR Board Risk Committee be notified within 48 hours of any demand crossing the threshold. The protocol must be tested in a tabletop exercise modelled on the ZF $70 million lump-sum demand scenario no later than 90 days post-ratification.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control's brief references the $70 million lump-sum threshold in general terms; this adaptation converts that reference into a dual-trigger monetary formula calibrated to JLR's sterling operating context, adds the intra-24-hour accountability chain that the Stellantis experience showed was absent, and mandates a live test rather than a paper policy.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must maintain a pre-approved roster of alternative
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supply Chain Resilience must, by 30 June 2026, maintain a pre-approved roster of at least two alternative or dual-qualified chassis module suppliers capable of supplying MLA-platform-compatible sub-assemblies to Solihull, with each alternative having passed JLR's technical homologation process and holding an active non-disclosure agreement and outline commercial terms with JLR, such that a stop-ship notice from any single Tier-1 chassis module supplier does not cause a production halt at Solihull exceeding 72 hours. The roster must be reviewed and re-validated quarterly, and the 72-hour ceiling must be evidenced by a logistics feasibility assessment — covering tooling portability, cross-border customs clearance from each alternative supplier's plant, and minimum order quantities — completed by JLR's logistics team and signed off by the CPO. Any gap between current roster capability and the 72-hour ceiling must be reported to the JLR Board as a named open risk until closed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The brief names the 72-hour ceiling and the MLA platform; this adaptation adds the specific homologation, logistics-feasibility, and customs-clearance evidence requirements that are necessary to make the roster operationally real rather than a list of nominally available suppliers, directly addressing the sole-dependency failure mode that halted Stellantis' Toluca plant for more than 11 days.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must include binding continuity-of-supply clauses — enforceable
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's General Counsel must ensure that every Tier-1 chassis module supply agreement renewed or entered into after 25 March 2026 contains a binding Continuity-of-Supply clause that: (a) is expressly governed by English law and, for suppliers incorporated in Michigan or Ontario, includes a parallel submission-to-jurisdiction clause for the relevant US or Canadian court; (b) defines 'payment dispute' as any demand by the supplier for a lump-sum payment or price increase exceeding the agreed contractual variation mechanism; (c) requires the supplier to continue shipping to JLR's Solihull, Halewood, and Nitra facilities without interruption for a minimum of 60 days following written notice of any payment dispute, during which period both parties must engage a named dispute-resolution mechanism; and (d) entitles JLR to seek injunctive relief without bond in the governing jurisdiction if the supplier initiates a stop-ship. Compliance with this requirement must be confirmed by external English-law counsel before any renewed agreement is countersigned, and a clause-conformance register must be maintained by the JLR Legal team and reviewed at each Procurement Board meeting.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control's brief flags the need for English-law and mirrored-jurisdiction enforceability; this adaptation specifies the exact substantive content the clause must contain — including the 60-day continuity floor derived from the Stellantis timeline and the no-bond injunction entitlement modelled on the Michigan TRO — rather than leaving clause content to individual negotiators.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must conduct enhanced due diligence on ZF
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Risk team must, within 60 days of 25 March 2026, complete enhanced due diligence on ZF Friedrichshafen AG and its joint-venture supply entities — specifically ZF Foxconn Chassis Modules (formerly ZF Chassis Modules Windsor) — covering: (a) the financial covenants and inter-company funding arrangements within the ZF Foxconn joint venture that could generate working-capital pressure sufficient to motivate a stop-ship ultimatum against JLR; (b) the operational and legal posture ZF Foxconn has established following the Michigan lawsuit and TRO of 25 March 2026, including any board-level resolutions regarding customer payment enforcement; and (c) ZF Friedrichshafen's parent-group liquidity position in the context of its publicly announced 14,000 job-cut programme through 2030. The enhanced due-diligence report must include a named risk rating for JLR's exposure, be reviewed by the CPO and CFO, and determine whether JLR should renegotiate payment terms or seek contractual protections within its existing ZF supply agreements within 90 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses due diligence on ZF's joint-venture entities generically; this adaptation directs the review at the specific financial mechanics — inter-company funding within the ZF Foxconn JV — that the Stellantis case revealed as the proximate driver of stop-ship willingness, linking it to ZF Friedrichshafen's documented restructuring pressure as a parent-group context factor.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must map all production lines at Solihull
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supply Chain Resilience must complete, within 45 days of 25 March 2026, a production-line dependency map for Solihull, Halewood, and Nitra that identifies every Tier-1 supplier that is a joint venture, subsidiary, or branded division of ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Robert Bosch GmbH, or Foxconn Industrial Internet, cross-referenced against each parent group's publicly announced restructuring programme (ZF: 14,000 job cuts by 2030; Bosch: 22,000 job cuts). The map must record, for each identified supplier: the specific component or module supplied, the production line(s) at risk, whether the relationship is single-source, and whether the parent group's restructuring creates a financially credible motivation to seek price renegotiation or lump-sum payment from JLR within the next 24 months. The completed map must be presented to the JLR Supply Chain Risk Committee and refreshed semi-annually, with any newly identified single-source dependency from these three parent groups escalated immediately to the CPO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control calls for a mapping exercise across the three parent groups; this adaptation makes the output actionable by requiring a restructuring-motivation assessment for each identified entity — converting the map from a static inventory into a forward-looking risk signal calibrated to the financial pressures that drove ZF's stop-ship posture toward Stellantis.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must require quarterly financial-health certifications from single-source
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Risk team must, within 60 days of 25 March 2026, implement a mandatory quarterly financial-health certification regime for all single-source module suppliers to the Solihull MLA and Halewood platforms, requiring each such supplier to submit: (a) unaudited management accounts no older than 45 days; (b) a signed representation that no lump-sum payment demand or price-renegotiation request outside the agreed contractual variation mechanism is being prepared or anticipated for the coming quarter; and (c) disclosure of any change in inter-company funding arrangements with the supplier's parent group. Certifications must be reviewed by JLR's Supplier Risk team within 10 business days of receipt, and any supplier whose accounts show a deterioration in gross margin exceeding 5 percentage points year-on-year — the type of margin compression that preceded ZF's December 2025 price-increase concession from Stellantis — must be immediately escalated to a watch-list with a mandated CPO review within 15 business days. Non-submission of a certification within 5 business days of the quarterly deadline must itself be treated as an escalation trigger.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control frames the certification regime around detecting deteriorating margin positions; this adaptation adds the specific financial metric trigger (5-point gross margin deterioration) and the inter-company funding disclosure requirement, both derived from the ZF case's pattern of margin pressure leading to escalating payment demands, and treats non-submission as an independent risk signal.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must update its Solihull and Halewood business-continuity
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Business Continuity must, within 60 days of 25 March 2026, update the Business Continuity Plans for Solihull and Halewood to incorporate a named scenario designated 'Tier-1 Payment Dispute — Stop-Ship' with the Stellantis Toluca 11-day halt (14 March to at least 25 March 2026) as the baseline duration assumption and a 22-day planning horizon. The scenario-specific response playbook must define: (a) the decision authority and communication cascade for the first 72 hours, including who authorises drawing on buffer inventory, who engages legal hold, and who notifies the JLR Board and Tata Motors; (b) the workforce management protocol for approximately 6,000 Solihull and relevant Halewood employees during a halt, including the interface with TULRCA obligations; and (c) the customer-communication and delivery-schedule-revision procedure for JLR's order bank. The updated BCPs must be reviewed and signed off by JLR's COO and tested in a tabletop exercise — using the ZF stop-ship scenario as the stimulus — within 90 days of sign-off.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control calls for BCPs to include a payment-dispute halt scenario; this adaptation mandates the precise internal structure of the playbook — role-by-role, hour-by-hour for the first 72 hours — and anchors the planning horizon to the actual Toluca duration, preventing the BCP from treating this as a hypothetical rather than a calibrated operational precedent.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must pre-position buffer inventory of chassis module
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Head of Supply Chain Operations must, by 30 June 2026, establish and physically verify a buffer inventory of chassis module sub-assemblies at Solihull equivalent to a minimum five production days' consumption at current MLA platform build rates, stored in a location that does not depend on the continued operation of the supplying Tier-1's EDI or call-off systems. The buffer must be calculated on a per-line-item basis covering all chassis module components for which JLR currently has a single-source Tier-1 dependency, must be replenished to the five-day minimum within 72 hours of any drawdown, and must be physically counted and reconciled against the target monthly by the Solihull Plant Director. JLR's CPO must receive a monthly inventory-compliance dashboard, and any buffer falling below three days' supply must trigger an automatic escalation to the CPO and Head of Supply Chain Resilience within 24 hours, modelling the notice-period exposure created when ZF initiated its stop-ship action against Stellantis on or around 12 March 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control specifies a five-day buffer in general terms; this adaptation adds the per-line-item scoping requirement, the 72-hour replenishment obligation, the monthly physical count, and the three-day early-warning escalation trigger — all derived from the operational reality that ZF's stop-ship began with a short notice period that left Stellantis without time to source alternatives before the Toluca line halted.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must disclose in its next Tata Motors
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's CFO must ensure that JLR's next Tata Motors consolidated reporting submission — and no later than the reporting cycle covering Q1 FY2027 — includes a specific disclosure of: (a) the October 2025 early-payment financing scheme introduced during JLR's manufacturing restart, including the total facility size, current utilisation, and the identities of Tier-1 supplier categories enrolled; (b) any lump-sum payment demands received from Tier-1 suppliers in the 12 months to the reporting date that exceeded £10 million or that were resolved through an unplanned cash payment; and (c) the maximum aggregate lump-sum exposure JLR would face if all single-source Tier-1 chassis module suppliers simultaneously demanded renegotiation at a scale proportionate to ZF's $70 million demand against Stellantis. The disclosure must be reviewed by JLR's external auditors and signed off by the CFO and General Counsel before inclusion in the Tata Motors submission.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires disclosure of working-capital arrangements that could be stressed by a ZF-style demand; this adaptation specifies the three concrete disclosure elements — scheme parameters, historical demands, and aggregate worst-case exposure — that would give Tata Motors' consolidated reporting the visibility needed to assess the financial risk, rather than leaving disclosure scope to management discretion.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must stress-test its revolving credit facilities against
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's CFO and Treasurer must, within 45 days of 25 March 2026, commission and present to the JLR Board Finance Committee a stress-test of JLR's revolving credit facilities modelling the simultaneous occurrence of: (a) a single Tier-1 chassis module supplier demanding a lump-sum cash payment of £55 million (the sterling equivalent of ZF's reported $70 million demand against Stellantis); (b) the October 2025 early-payment financing scheme remaining fully drawn at its current utilisation level; and (c) a production halt at Solihull of 11 days' duration — matching the confirmed Toluca halt duration — generating lost-revenue and fixed-cost impacts to be quantified by JLR's FP&A team. The stress-test output must state whether JLR's available undrawn RCF headroom remains sufficient to meet the lump-sum demand without breaching any financial covenant, and must identify the specific covenant(s) at risk. If headroom is insufficient under the base stress scenario, the CFO must present a remediation plan — including RCF upsizing, demand deferral mechanisms, or alternative financing — to the Board Finance Committee within a further 30 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets the £55 million sterling threshold and references the early-payment scheme; this adaptation adds the concurrent 11-day lost-revenue shock and the specific covenant-breach test, converting the stress exercise from a single-variable sensitivity into a compound scenario that reflects the actual combined financial pressure the Stellantis situation demonstrated can arise simultaneously.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: JLR must confirm with UK employment law counsel
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's General Counsel must, within 30 days of 25 March 2026, obtain written advice from external UK employment law counsel confirming: (a) at what point a Tier-1-supplier-triggered production halt at Solihull — employing approximately 6,000 workers — would cross the 100-employee threshold for collective redundancy consultation under TULRCA 1992 s.188, including whether temporary layoff or short-time working arrangements during a payment-dispute halt constitute 'proposals to dismiss' for the purposes of s.188; (b) the minimum 45-day consultation period that would apply and the consequences of non-compliance; and (c) the earliest date by which HR and the CPO must initiate any required notification to employee representatives if a halt commences without prior warning, to avoid liability. The written advice must be circulated to JLR's COO, CPO, HR Director, and Board, and must be appended as a standing annex to the 'Tier-1 Payment Dispute — Stop-Ship' BCP scenario required under AC-OPS-029-PSP-01, so that the s.188 clock and the operational response timeline are managed in parallel from day one of any future halt.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control identifies the TULRCA s.188 threshold question; this adaptation directs the inquiry at the specific uncertainty — whether temporary layoff during a payment-dispute halt (as distinct from a redundancy programme) triggers s.188 consultation obligations — and integrates the legal advice directly into the BCP playbook so that the employment-law timeline is built into the operational response rather than addressed reactively.

incidents.headline: Aptiv splits — Versigent / Cyprium $1.5B notes and 12–24 months of contract turbulence
incidents.summary: Aptiv PLC commenced a $1.5B private notes offering by Cyprium Corp / Cyprium Lux — subsidiaries of Versigent, the new holding company for Aptiv's Electrical Distribution Systems segment, to be spun off to shareholders. Aptiv's 8-K explicitly cites geopolitical and Ukraine/Middle East risks in the same filing. 12–24 months of contract-administration turbulence on Aptiv-supplied JLR programmes. ERP cutover risk during separation. Two counterparties where one existed. The risk is not Aptiv. The risk is the precedent: when a Tier-1 splits, Kass's vendor risk dossier inherits or doesn't inherit the audit trail. JLR's master data team will spend 12–24 months re-papering EDS contracts against two counterparties where one existed. The strategic question is whether spin-offs from Tier-1s should be treated as fresh-onboarding vendors requiring full re-test — or whether legacy audit history carries across. The risk register does not name this distinction.

graded_claims.claim: Aptiv's Board of Directors approved the spin-off of its Electrical Distribution Systems business into a new publicly traded company, Versigent, effective April 1, 2026.
Cyprium Corporation and Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg (subsidiaries of Versigent) issued senior notes, originally sized at $1.5 billion and upsized to $1.6 billion, closing on March 18, 2026.
Versigent and Aptiv entered into a Separation and Distribution Agreement, a Transition Services Agreement, a Tax Matters Agreement, an Employee Matters Agreement, and an IP Cross License Agreement.
Aptiv shareholders received one Versigent share for every three Aptiv shares held as of the March 17, 2026 record date.
Versigent co-issuers also secured an $850 million revolving credit facility and a $500 million term loan facility alongside the notes offering.
Aptiv's 8-K filings cite geopolitical, Ukraine, and Middle East risks in the same separation-related filings.
JLR's Aptiv-supplied wiring harness programmes will require 12-24 months of contract re-papering against two counterparties where one previously existed.
ERP cutover risk during the Aptiv-to-Versigent separation creates system-continuity exposure for downstream customers.
The separation agreement terms may not be as favourable to Versigent as agreements negotiated with independent third parties.

internal.recommended_action_for_them: Convene a cross-functional EDS contract triage team within 72 hours to map every Aptiv purchase-order line to the correct Versigent or Aptiv successor entity.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure JLR's vendor risk programme identifies Versigent as a new legal entity requiring full third-party due-diligence assessment, not an inherited continuation of the legacy Aptiv EDS audit trail.
Maintain continuous operational-risk monitoring across Versigent's Transition Services Agreement period, specifically covering ERP cutover milestones that could interrupt JLR wiring-harness supply.
Define and test contract-continuity controls governing how JLR purchase orders transfer from Aptiv to Versigent as legal successor, including indemnity, IP, and quality-obligation novation.
Establish contractual obligations binding Versigent to programme-delivery KPIs and audit-access rights equivalent to those held against Aptiv prior to the April 1, 2026 distribution date.

Assess strategic supply-base concentration risk arising from the Aptiv-to-Versigent split, including whether JLR's EDS single-source positions now sit with a more leveraged, less-tested standalone entity.
Evaluate Versigent's post-spin capital structure — $1.6B notes plus $1.35B credit facilities — against JLR programme revenue dependencies to assess counterparty financial-health risk.

clean_claims.claim: Aptiv's Board of Directors approved the spin-off of its Electrical Distribution Systems business into a new publicly traded company, Versigent, effective April 1, 2026.
Cyprium Corporation and Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg (subsidiaries of Versigent) issued senior notes, originally sized at $1.5 billion and upsized to $1.6 billion, closing on March 18, 2026.
Versigent and Aptiv entered into a Separation and Distribution Agreement, a Transition Services Agreement, a Tax Matters Agreement, an Employee Matters Agreement, and an IP Cross License Agreement.
Aptiv shareholders received one Versigent share for every three Aptiv shares held as of the March 17, 2026 record date.
Versigent co-issuers also secured an $850 million revolving credit facility and a $500 million term loan facility alongside the notes offering.
Aptiv's 8-K filings cite geopolitical, Ukraine, and Middle East risks in the same separation-related filings.
The separation agreement terms may not be as favourable to Versigent as agreements negotiated with independent third parties.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Trigger a fresh Versigent vendor-onboarding assessment under JLR's
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Third-Party Risk function must open a net-new vendor onboarding case for Versigent in JLR's supplier risk management system by May 31, 2026, treating the April 1, 2026 legal separation date as the supplier inception event and explicitly flagging that no legacy Aptiv risk ratings, audit scores, or due-diligence conclusions carry forward to the Versigent entity without independent re-validation. The onboarding assessment must cover financial health (including the $1.6B Cyprium senior notes and $1.35B credit facilities disclosed in the separation filings), operational continuity, and quality system standing, and must be signed off by the JLR Head of Supplier Risk before any Versigent invoice is processed. Completion evidence is a fully populated Versigent vendor record in the risk system, a dated sign-off memo, and a gap analysis comparing the Versigent assessment against the last Aptiv assessment cycle.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires fresh onboarding for new third parties; this adaptation captures the specific risk that Aptiv's existing approved-supplier status could be administratively inherited by Versigent by default in JLR's systems, bypassing the re-assessment gate entirely — a failure mode made acute by the April 1, 2026 legal cut-over date and the material change in Versigent's standalone financial profile relative to Aptiv.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Validate that all Aptiv-issued quality conformance records
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Quality Engineering team must, by June 30, 2026, compile a full register of every PPAP package, IATF 16949 certificate, and quality conformance record held against Aptiv EDS for JLR wiring-harness programmes and confirm in writing — with documentary evidence from Versigent's quality organisation — that each record has been formally novated or reissued in Versigent's legal name and remains valid under the applicable certification body. Any PPAP or certificate that cannot be confirmed as novated must be escalated to JLR's Chief Procurement Officer as a production-continuity risk and treated as requiring fresh part qualification under JLR's APQP process. Completion evidence is a signed novation register, copies of reissued or novated certificates, and a written confirmation from Versigent's quality director that no JLR-relevant certificate lapses during the Transition Services Agreement window.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses quality-record continuity; this adaptation names the specific documentation types (PPAP, IATF 16949) and the precise legal failure mode — records remaining in Aptiv's name after the April 1, 2026 separation — which would render JLR non-compliant with its own APQP and customer-specific requirements if Versigent ships parts against lapsed or misattributed quality approvals.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Map every active Aptiv EDS purchase order in
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's SAP procurement team, led by the Master Data Management owner, must run a full extraction of all open Aptiv EDS purchase orders in SAP by April 30, 2026 and produce a line-by-line mapping confirming the successor legal entity (Versigent or the relevant Cyprium subsidiary) for each order, with SAP vendor master records updated to reflect the substitution before any Versigent invoice is approved for payment. Any purchase order where legal-entity substitution cannot be confirmed from the Separation and Distribution Agreement schedules must be quarantined and referred to JLR Legal for novation before the invoice hold is released. Completion evidence is a dated SAP extraction report, a reconciliation log showing old and new vendor codes, and a sign-off from the JLR P2P Controller confirming no Versigent invoices have been approved against an Aptiv vendor record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control covers purchase-order mapping; this adaptation identifies the specific system (JLR's SAP procurement module), the exact legal-entity substitution risk created by the Separation and Distribution Agreement's April 1, 2026 effective date, and the payment-default exposure that arises if invoices from the new Cyprium-subsidiary obligors are matched against legacy Aptiv vendor codes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require Versigent to provide JLR with an ERP-readiness
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Procurement Operations Director must, by May 15, 2026, formally request from Versigent a written ERP-readiness attestation — signed by a CFO-level officer within Versigent's finance organisation — confirming that the SAP or equivalent ERP cutover from Aptiv's systems to Versigent's standalone environment will not interrupt order acknowledgement, delivery scheduling, or invoicing for any active JLR wiring-harness programme during the Transition Services Agreement window. The attestation must specify the planned cutover date, the fallback procedure if cutover is delayed, and the Versigent contact accountable for JLR programme continuity during the transition. If the attestation is not received by May 15, 2026, JLR's Head of Supplier Risk must escalate to the Chief Procurement Officer and initiate a contingency stock-build review for affected part numbers. Completion evidence is the signed attestation on file and a dated escalation log if the deadline is missed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses ERP continuity; this adaptation operationalises the specific risk flagged by the Transition Services Agreement structure — that Versigent will rely on Aptiv's systems under TSA for a defined period, creating a deferred cutover risk that could silently disrupt JLR order management — and anchors accountability to a named CFO-level signatory rather than a generic IT team.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Insert a Versigent-specific change-of-control and spin-off notification clause
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR Legal must ensure that every re-papered JLR EDS master supply agreement executed with Versigent — or any Cyprium Corporation or Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg entity acting as the contracting obligor — includes a change-of-control and further-restructuring notification clause requiring a minimum 90 calendar days' written notice to JLR's Chief Procurement Officer before any corporate transaction that changes the identity, ownership, or debt-issuer status of the Cyprium co-issuers or effects a transfer of the EDS business to a third party. The clause must explicitly reference the $1.6B senior notes issued by Cyprium Corporation and Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg as a trigger event baseline, such that any refinancing, covenant amendment, or new debt issuance above $100M also triggers a 30-day notice obligation. Completion evidence is JLR Legal's confirmation that the clause appears in all executed Versigent MSAs and a clause-audit certificate signed by the JLR General Counsel or delegated legal director.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires change-of-control notification provisions; this adaptation names the specific issuer entities (Cyprium Corporation and Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg) whose debt structure was confirmed in the March 2026 offering and whose further restructuring would represent exactly the compounding Tier-1 fragmentation risk the incident identifies, requiring the clause to be calibrated to Versigent's actual capital structure rather than a boilerplate threshold.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a Versigent concentration-risk review quantifying JLR's single-source
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Supplier Risk and Procurement Strategy teams must jointly complete, by July 31, 2026, a concentration-risk quantification review mapping every active wiring-harness part number supplied by Aptiv EDS / Versigent to the JLR vehicle platform it supports, and calculating the annual spend and sole-source exposure attributable to Versigent per platform. The review must benchmark Versigent's post-spin leverage — confirmed at approximately $2.35B in total debt comprising the $1.6B Cyprium senior notes, $850M revolving credit facility, and $500M term loan — against the leverage thresholds at which JLR's supplier risk policy requires dual-source qualification, and must produce a red/amber/green rating for each platform based on that benchmark. The review output must be presented to the JLR Chief Procurement Officer and documented in JLR's supplier risk register as the Versigent concentration baseline. Completion evidence is the completed platform exposure map, the leverage benchmarking calculation, and the CPO sign-off record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires concentration-risk review for leveraged suppliers; this adaptation quantifies the specific debt quantum confirmed in the Cyprium notes offering and credit facilities, maps it to JLR's actual platform dependency on EDS wiring harnesses, and requires a risk rating output tied to JLR's existing dual-source qualification thresholds — none of which the generic control names.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update JLR's supplier-exit and business-continuity plans to reflect
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Business Continuity and Procurement Strategy functions must update JLR's supplier exit and business-continuity plans by August 31, 2026 to reflect Versigent's standalone risk profile as an unrated, leveraged entity carrying approximately $2.35B in debt with no Aptiv investment-grade balance sheet backstop, and must test the feasibility of sourcing the top five JLR wiring-harness part numbers from alternative qualified suppliers within a 90-day mobilisation window. The feasibility test must document for each part number: the current alternative-source qualification status, the estimated lead time and tooling cost to activate an alternative, and any single-tooling or single-die constraints that would prevent substitution within 90 days. Results must be reviewed by the JLR Head of Supply Chain Risk and any part number assessed as infeasible for 90-day substitution must be escalated to the CPO with a recommended mitigation (safety stock increase, expedited dual-source qualification, or platform re-design review). Completion evidence is the updated BCP document, the feasibility test log, and the CPO escalation record where applicable.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires BCP updates for distressed suppliers; this adaptation grounds the update in Versigent's confirmed debt structure from the March 2026 notes offering, specifies the 90-day feasibility test horizon tied to realistic automotive supply-chain requalification timelines, and requires a part-number-level infeasibility escalation path that the generic control does not prescribe.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Present to JLR Board a strategic supply-base review
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Chief Procurement Officer must present to the JLR Board (or its designated Supply Chain Risk sub-committee) by Q3 2026 a strategic supply-base review addressing whether Tier-1 supplier spin-offs — using the Aptiv/Versigent separation effective April 1, 2026 as the governing precedent case — should be classified as mandatory dual-source qualification triggers under JLR's supply strategy framework. The review must assess at minimum: (a) the number of other JLR Tier-1 suppliers whose corporate structures create plausible spin-off risk within a three-year horizon; (b) the adequacy of current JLR master supply agreement change-of-control clauses to capture spin-off scenarios distinct from traditional M&A; and (c) a recommended policy amendment or new policy instrument specifically addressing Tier-1 spin-offs. The Board presentation must include a draft decision record for approval or deferral with stated rationale. Completion evidence is the Board paper, the meeting minutes recording the decision, and a dated policy amendment submission if the Board approves the recommendation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a strategic supply-base review; this adaptation uses the confirmed April 1, 2026 Aptiv/Versigent separation as the explicit precedent case to anchor Board-level deliberation on a gap the incident exposes — that JLR's existing risk register and MSA change-of-control clauses do not distinguish between spin-offs and conventional M&A — requiring the review to produce a policy instrument, not merely an observation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Obtain Versigent's audited opening-balance-sheet financials and the Cyprium
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Finance and Treasury function must, by July 31, 2026, obtain Versigent's audited opening-balance-sheet financials (as filed following the April 1, 2026 separation) and the full Cyprium Corporation and Cyprium Holdings Luxembourg senior note indenture, and must complete a stress-test modelling Versigent's leverage covenant headroom under three scenarios — base, automotive-downturn, and geopolitical disruption (consistent with the risk language cited in Aptiv's separation-related 8-K filings) — to assess the probability and timing of a covenant breach within the first 18 months post-spin. The stress-test must quantify the JLR supply-continuity exposure in each scenario (days of wiring-harness inventory cover, estimated production lines at risk, financial impact) and must be reviewed by JLR's CFO. Any scenario producing a modelled covenant breach probability above a threshold agreed by the CFO must trigger immediate escalation to the concentration-risk review under AC-TPR-024-SPO-01. Completion evidence is the stress-test model, the CFO review sign-off, and any escalation record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires financial stress-testing of leveraged suppliers; this adaptation names the specific debt instruments (Cyprium senior notes confirmed at $1.6B, revolving credit facility at $850M, term loan at $500M), anchors the geopolitical scenario dimension to the confirmed 8-K risk language, and creates an explicit escalation bridge to the concentration-risk control, operationalising the otherwise abstract covenant-breach monitoring obligation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review JLR's supply-chain finance arrangements referencing Aptiv as named obligor
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: JLR's Treasury and Accounts Payable teams must, by April 30, 2026, identify every supply-chain finance arrangement, reverse-factoring facility, or early-payment programme in which Aptiv EDS is named as the obligor or approved seller, and must either novate or formally amend those arrangements to substitute Versigent (or the relevant Cyprium entity) as the named obligor before the next JLR invoice cycle following the April 1, 2026 separation. Any facility where the financing bank or programme administrator requires its own credit approval of Versigent as a new obligor must be flagged to JLR's Head of Treasury immediately, and JLR must not approve payment under that facility until the bank's substitution consent is confirmed in writing, to avoid a payment-default risk arising from entity mismatch between the contracting party and the named obligor. Completion evidence is a register of all affected SCF facilities, novation or amendment confirmations from each programme administrator, and a Treasury sign-off confirming no Versigent invoices have been settled under a facility still naming Aptiv.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses SCF facility novation for entity changes; this adaptation specifies the April 1, 2026 cut-over date as the hard deadline, identifies the secondary credit-approval risk that arises when a financing bank must independently approve Versigent — an unrated, newly leveraged entity — as a substitute obligor, and closes the gap where a payment could be processed under a mismatched facility name before the bank consents, creating a default event.

incidents.headline: Agratas as single source — JLR's BEV future routes through one Tata-owned JV with no contractual leverage
incidents.summary: JLR's BEV strategy depends on a single qualified battery-cell source: Agratas, a Tata-owned joint venture. No alternative supplier is qualified at JLR's vehicle-architecture volume in any region. Agratas Somerset (Gravity Smart Campus) start-of-production has already slipped from late 2026 to late 2027; industry signal points to late 2028. The £500M+ UK government grant programme architecture-locks the configuration — JLR cannot pivot the cell chemistry or supplier without re-opening the grant terms. Range Rover Electric volume cadence is already reduced by approximately one-third versus the November 2025 product plan — slip evidenced, not hypothetical. Tata Sons capital-allocation pressure (Air India re-financing, Tata Power CapEx, Tata Capital IPO) is now competing with Agratas tranche-3 funding schedule. JLR has parent-relationship alignment with the supplier but no contractual mechanism to enforce SOP commitments — the legal architecture of the JV does not include OEM customer remedies for delay. Eleven cards in this deck stress-test risks JLR's register names. This one stress-tests the dependency the register does not name. If Somerset slips a second time, the BEV launch cadence for Range Rover Electric, the all-electric Jaguar GT, and every JLR brand BEV by 2030 is no longer a JLR decision — it is a Tata Sons capital-allocation decision. The register names the Electrification Transition as a new principal risk. It does not separately name the single-source concentration that determines whether the transition lands. The chip row below carries the verdict: the register has the line, it does not have the dependency.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure JLR's BEV product plan is not structurally gated by a single Tata-group cell supplier without contractual SOP-enforcement remedies or a qualified alternative source.
Ensure the Agratas JV governance structure is reviewed to determine whether intra-group alignment constitutes adequate M&A / partnership risk management under JLR's principal risk framework.
Ensure JLR's third-party risk programme explicitly covers Agratas as a concentration risk, not merely a related-party, given Tata Sons' competing capital-allocation demands in FY2026-27.
Ensure JLR's electrification supply-chain operations have documented contingency plans covering an Agratas Somerset SOP slip of 12 months or more beyond the current late-2027 target.

Ensure the geographic concentration of JLR's entire BEV cell supply in a single UK-located Tata-group facility is explicitly named in the JLR Principal Risk Register as a geographic single-point-of-failure.

Ensure the UK government £380M grant conditionality does not foreclose JLR's ability to qualify a geographically diversified secondary cell supplier without requiring full re-negotiation of grant terms.
